This dashboard has a variety of 
displays depicting performance 
measurements to help make 
decisions.

Infographics
Some users have a difficult time getting information from tables. Either users “get lost” in all of 
the numbers or they see so many tables that they get tired of looking at them. Graphs and charts 
are effective, but the trend is to combine numbers, charts, and different types of graphs together 
in one view at the same time in an infographic.
The term infographic can mean any graph, chart, or picture that communicates the data better 
than words or tables. To the designer, it means visually displaying data for the user to see and 
choosing which type of graph or chart, or even which numbers, are most appropriate.
Designers have many options for creating an infographic page either on paper or on a web 
page. One web-based application for making infographics is called Piktochart (https://piktochart.
com). An example of an infographic developed with Picktochart software is shown in Figure 11.9. 
It depicts data about software adoption using many visual elements including icons, a pie chart, an 
area graph, text, and customer rankings from 1 to 5 as stars. Customer satisfaction is also pictured 
FIGURE 11.9
An infographic using a variety of 
design elements to visually pres-
ent data about customer software 
adoption over time.

## PAGE 372
336    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
by the extent of the frown or smile the character is sporting. Notice the artist has used a limited 
color palette of red, teal, and black against a buff-colored background. Red is a high-energy color 
that attracts attention to important information on the infographic.
Infographics should be newsworthy. Start the infographic with a headline, something brief 
but catchy. Then artfully design a small page of information and mix in visuals that together tell a 
story. These design elements include charts and graphs, images (mostly icons and cartoons—only 
rarely should photos be used), summary information in brief text, and contact information or 
some action the reader might want to take. The most viewed and shared content on social media 
is visual, so it makes sense to design infographics to convey content you want shared.
DATA VISUALIZATION
Data visualization is the representation of data in the form of a chart, diagram, map, or other 
visual. It is used to make complex data understandable. For many people, seeing data displayed in 
a chart or map is easier to comprehend than if the same data were displayed in a table. Commercial 
data visualization packages are available for Tableau, QlikView, and Zoho.
An interesting site for data visualizations is hosted by Datawrapper. In addition to the tools 
available for data visualizations, Datawrapper hosts a community-based site called “The River.” 
They describe it as a place to exchange charts and other graphics with other Datawrapper users. 
Anyone can visit The River, find a visualization, customize the chart in Datawrapper, and publish 
it to their website. Examples from the River can be found in Figure 11.10.
There are organizations that collect data. These sources include The World Bank, World 
Health Organization (WHO), International Monetary Fund (IMF), and assorted nongovernmental 
organizations (NGOs). There are also people—for example, journalists—who report on this data. 
The problem is that creating charts and graphs is time-consuming. Data must be downloaded from 
the collector, processed using Microsoft Excel, R, or other data visualization app, then designed 
(selecting the size of the chart and margins, choosing fonts, writing titles, and adding footnotes). 
The River allows the reporter to find an appropriate style and get the visualization up and running 
as quickly and effortlessly as possible.
The data visualizations posted on The River make it possible to go well beyond basic 
charts and graphs to provide enhanced graphics efficiently. Enhanced graphics can show more 
variables and more types of variables on a single diagram. Furthermore, the software can identify 
trends, patterns, and even reasons for outliers in data. This makes data visualization a very  
powerful tool.
Some advantages of using data visualization are:
1. Viewing data in a better way
2. Identifying trends and patterns
3. Seeing more than one attribute at a time
4. Making decisions quickly
5. Communicating information to others in a more effective manner
R has become one of the most commonly used languages in data mining. It is an open 
source and free-to-use language that is suitable for data visualizations. The British Broadcasting 
Company (BBC) has created a package and instructions for drawing graphics similar to the ones 
used in BBC reports. It will take a lot of practice to achieve the skills that created the graphics; 
however, the tutorials are worthwhile.
Here are some dos and don’ts regarding visualization. First, here are the dos:
1. Do choose the right graphic.
2. Do use color wisely.
3. Do plot different variables or attributes on the same diagram if possible.
4. Do realize that various customers will perceive the visualization differently.
And here are some of the don’ts:
1. Don’t choose the wrong style graphic.
2. Don’t turn visualization into an infographic.
3. Don’t introduce bias into the visualization.
4. Don’t expect everyone to prefer the visualization you created.

## PAGE 373
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     337
FIGURE 11.10
The River, by Datawrapper, is a place where data visualizations can be reused by those who want to show statistics graphically.  
(Courtesy: Datawrapper)
Data Visualization and the NYC Subway System
Assume that not everyone will understand or appreciate the graphics you create through data 
visualization. Take, for example, the Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA) maps designed for 
the New York City subway system, a system of trains that delivers almost 6 million rides daily. 
Its extensive system map has been redesigned many times.
The last major design update was made in 1979 to replace a beautifully drawn abstract map 
from 1972 that received many complaints because the geography was distorted to give the map of 
subway lines a cleaner look. The problem was that it made stations look closer than they were, 
and it was difficult to determine which station was closer to a rider’s destination.
In designing the 1979 maps, it is reported that the designers rode the entire subway system 
with their eyes closed so they could feel the curves. The map (see Figure 11.11) incorporates those 
curves as well as clues to orienting oneself above ground. For example, the lakes (in blue, still

## PAGE 374
338    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
FIGURE 11.11
The current Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA) map, originally designed in 1979 for the New York City subway system, featured curves to 
help riders orient themselves. (Metropolitan Transportation Authority)
FIGURE 11.12
The new experimental Metropolitan Transit Authority subway map depicts subway lines as sharp angles and shows only minimal 
geographic details. Will riders prefer this new angular design to the existing design? (Metropolitan Transportation Authority)

## PAGE 375
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     339
FIGURE 11.13
The new interactive Metropolitan Transit Authority subway map can do more than can be accomplished on a paper map as it orients the 
map for a particular rider. (Metropolitan Transportation Authority)
misshapen) in Central Park (in green and identified by name) helps riders determine which station 
to use and to orient themselves once they ascend the stairs to street level. Much of the map was 
still distorted, but the 1979 map was viewed more favorably by New Yorkers (Genzlinger, 2020).
The map was a success, even though it broke a lot of the conventional wisdom of design. For 
example, it is suggested that a design uses three or four font styles, yet there are about 20 different 
font styles in various sizes, colors, weights, and kerning (the spacing between characters) on the MTA 
map. Somehow it works and with a few tweaks here and there, the map survived for over 40 years.
As this book is being written, MTA has rolled out a new map (shown in Figure 11.12) and is 
asking for feedback. At this time, we don’t know whether it will be accepted by New Yorkers. You 
may first notice that Central Park is still there in green, but the lakes do not appear. The other very 
noticeable aspect is the way the subway lines are depicted. In this new map, subway lines R, W, 
N, and Q are each represented by a line. In the 1979 map, these four lines would be represented 
by one “trunk” line. Curves have become sharper angles.
MTA also introduced a new digitally interactive subway map in 2020, shown in Figure 11.13, 
and this may or may not be MTA’s biggest success (Goldbaum, 2020). The map, created by  
Work & Co on a pro bono basis, is geographically correct, but because it’s interactive, the map 
reconfigures itself based on the needs of the rider. A rider can see where they are at a given 
moment on an expanded view of the subway line they are riding.
The map is certainly valuable to visitors to the city and to everyone who needs to know 
whether a subway line is not functioning, but will it be more useful than posted signs, maps, and 
Google or Apple maps that are in use today? We think that depends on the improvements that will 
follow. We still need a separate app to tell us which car on a long train will open up its doors to 
the closest station staircase to our destination.
Expect that data visualizations will continue to improve people’s lives in many ways.
DESIGNING A WEBSITE
Borrow some design principles from other displays when designing a website. Remember, though, 
that the key word here is site. The first documents displayed on the internet using the HTTP pro-
tocol were called home pages, but it became apparent very quickly that companies, universities,

## PAGE 376
340    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
governments, and people were not going to be displaying just one page. The term website replaced 
home page in many instances, indicating that the array of pages would have to be organized, 
coordinated, designed, developed, and maintained in an orderly process. Sometimes the initial 
web page the user lands on is referred to as a landing page.
Printing is a highly controlled medium, and the analyst has a very good idea of what the 
output will look like. Graphical user interface and character-based user interface screens are also 
highly controlled. The web, however, is a very uncontrolled environment for output.
Responsive Web Design
Different browsers display images differently, and screen resolution has a large influence on the 
look and feel of a website. There is no “standard” resolution anymore. For many years displays 
had a 4:3 aspect ratio and common resolutions were 1024 × 768 pixels or 1600 × 1200 pixels. 
Now displays are made for HD and have a 16:9 aspect ratio. The issue is further complicated by 
the use of handheld devices, such as smartphones and tablets, that are used to browse the web. The 
complexity deepens when you realize that each person may set a browser to use different fonts  
and may disable the use of JavaScript, cookies, and other web programming elements. Analysts and 
users face many decisions when designing a website.
Responsive web design means that a website is developed so that it can be viewed on any 
device: a desktop, tablet, or smartphone. This includes seeing all the content, experiencing the 
same design concepts, and being able to perform all of the tasks on any device. Websites are 
designed in percentages rather than a fixed number of pixels.
Although a web designer may easily implement responsive web design for a particular com-
pany, problems may occur because advertising used on the website (banners, for example) may 
not be designed to be fluid. To overcome this, a web designer might need to develop different 
landing pages for each type of device.
Alternatively, Ajax can recognize a device and display different-sized advertising components 
on a page. Adaptive web design means that a different design layout is chosen to be displayed 
depending on the device. Adaptive design uses a fixed grid rather than the fluid (percentage) 
grid in responsive web design. Some designers think this approach is more flexible if the design 
and abilities of a particular device change over time.
Cloud-based approaches to web design such as Weebly or Wix use a drag-and-drop approach, 
and novice users can design a website quickly and without any knowledge of HTML code. They 
use a WYSIWYG (or “what you see is what you get”) interface. Both of these services are cur -
rently free but limited in features because they are based or a freemium model. Users can pay 
to have the companies obtain a domain name for them and host a website. Although it is easy to 
develop a website with these cloud-based approaches, transferring a developed site to another 
hosting company presents an obstacle.
Stand-alone web design development software is also available. These packages are full of 
features, have no advertising watermark, and are not host-specific. Working with stand-alone web 
design development software makes it is easier to move a website if the customer is not pleased 
with the service the hosting company is providing.
Flat Web Design
Recently, web design trends moved away from skeuomorphic design to flat web design. In skeuo-
morphic design, objects were meant to look realistic and three-dimensional, with shadows added 
to artwork. For example, a circle and a drop shadow would represent a button. This is fake realism. 
In addition, note pads would have spiral bindings, an address book would have rifled pages, and 
a blotter would represent a desktop. These images became time-worn representations of office 
elements not currently in use.
Flat web design, however, is a minimalistic approach to design. In comparison to skeuo-
morphic design, flat web design is clean, two-dimensional, and simple in many ways, including a 
bright color palette that is adhered to in all design. The idea behind this approach is to concentrate 
on only what is necessary. But it also makes it very clear that digital apps are distinct from their 
physical and tactile relatives.
Figure 11.14 compares the skeuomorphic icons of familiar objects, such as a calendar and 
a cell phone, with those same objects rendered in flat web design, which is an abstracted image 
that does not depict the shadows and contours of the physical world.

## PAGE 377
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     341
Flat web design’s popularity will make way for other web design approaches. We are begin-
ning to see a resurgence in realistic 3-D or skeuomorphic design. As you develop new apps and 
websites, keep searching for new design elements such as an emphasis on creative type fonts, 
textured backgrounds, illustrated sites, map-centered sites, and sites that have subtle motion.
General Guidelines for Designing Websites
In addition to the general design elements discussed earlier in this chapter, there are specific 
guidelines appropriate for the design of professional-quality websites:
Use Professional Tools. Consider using web editor software such as Adobe Dreamweaver or 
Website X5 Evolution (for Windows) or Rapidweaver (for Mac OS). These tools are definitely 
worth the price. You’ll be more creative and you’ll get the website finished much faster than if 
you work directly with hypertext markup language (HTML). Another option is to design a website 
using Weebly or Wix, but this approach might limit you from transferring the website to another 
hosting firm.
Study Other Websites. Look at websites you and other users think are engaging. Analyze what 
design elements are being used and see how they are functioning and then try to emulate what you 
see by creating prototype pages. (It is not ethical or legal to cut and paste pictures or code, but 
you can still learn from the other sites.)
Examine the Work of Professional Designers. As you look at professionally designed pages, 
ask yourself, “What works? What doesn’t work? In what ways can users interact with the site?” 
For example, does the site have hot links to email addresses, interactive forms to fill in, consumer 
surveys, games, quizzes, chat rooms, blogs, and so on? What about color schemes and pervasive 
metaphors?
Use the Tools You’ve Learned. Figure 11.15 shows a form web designers have used successfully 
to evaluate web pages systematically. You might want to use copies of the form to help compare 
and contrast the many websites you will visit as you go about learning web page design.
Use Storyboarding, Wireframing, and Mockups. Designing for the web brought back a prac-
tice known in the film industry as storyboarding. In the 1930s at Walt Disney Studios, each 
scene was drawn on a piece of paper and then tacked to a bulletin board in order of the episodes. 
(To see a fun and succinct animated version of storyboarding, check out https://disneyanimation 
.com/process/story/.)
In developing a website (or any app, for that matter), a storyboard could be used to show 
the differences between screens. It can show how a visitor to the site would navigate the website 
to find the information desired. Now storyboards can be developed in Microsoft PowerPoint or 
Apple’s Keynote by clicking on the slide organizer, and they can be drawn in Microsoft Visio 
or OmniGraffle as well.
Page design can be accomplished using a process called wireframing, which shows only the 
basics. There is no color, no type style. Graphics are shown as a simple box with an X drawn in 
it. In this way, each of the items acts as a placeholder.
FIGURE 11.14
Comparing skeuomorphic (3-D, 
realistic) icons with those drawn 
using flat web design.
Skeuomorphic
Design
Flat Design

## PAGE 378
342    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
Wireframing allows the designer to plan:
1. The overall design, showing what element appears at each position on the page
2. The navigational design, showing how to move from one page to the next using buttons, 
tabs, links, and pull-down menus
3. The interface design, showing how to interact with the website by inputting data or 
responding to questions
An alternative to a wireframe is a mockup. Mockups show what the output and input will 
look like before programming the underlying code to make the program or application functional.
Software that helps a systems designer develop a mockup is abundant. Built into the software 
are objects that can be dragged and dropped onto the screen. Templates are available for any type 
of display including desktops, notebooks, smartphones, and tablets. When designing for smart-
phones and tablets, both screen orientations are included. Both wireframing and mockups are a 
form of nonoperational prototyping discussed previously in Chapter 6.
Create Templates of Your Own. If you adopt a standard-looking page for most of the pages you 
create, you’ll get the website up and running quickly and it will consistently look good. Websites 
may be made using cascading style sheets (CSSs), a language that allows a designer to specify 
the color, font size, font type, and many other attributes only once. These attributes are stored in 
a style sheet file and applied to many web pages. If a designer changes a specification in the style 
sheet file, all the web pages using that style sheet will be updated to reflect the new style.
Use Extensions, Plug-Ins, Audio, and Video Sparingly. It is wonderful to include features that 
the professional pages have but remember that everyone looking at your site doesn’t have every 
new plug-in or extension. Don’t discourage visitors to your page.
Specific Guidelines for Website Design
Good websites are well thought out. Pay attention to the following:
1. Structure
2. Content

“This stuff looks terrible. It looks like scraps. My kindergar -
dener makes better reports in crayon. Look at it. It’s all ground up 
together. I can’t find anything. Where’s the summary of the number 
of beef items sold in each outlet? Where is the total volume of items 
sold for all outlets? How about the information on our own shop 
downtown?” says Kwon, slicing at the report.
The report clearly needs to be redesigned. Design a report (or 
reports) that better suits Ari Kwon. What approach can the analyst take 
in suggesting more reports with a less-crowded format? Use a paragraph 
to comment on the difficulty of implementing user suggestions that go 
against your design training. What are the trade-offs involved (as far as 
information overload goes) in generating numerous reports as opposed 
to generating one large report containing all the information Ari wants? 
Devise and write a heuristic (a rule of thumb) concerning the display 
of report information on one report in contrast to the generation of 
numerous reports. Consider advocating a web-based or app-based 
dashboard solution that would permit hyperlinks to all the information 
Ari desires. How feasible is that? Respond in a paragraph.
CONSULTING OPPORTUNITY 11.4 
Is Your Work a Grind?
“I want everything I can get my hands on, and the tighter the 
information is packed, the better. Forget that stuff you hear about 
information overload. It’s not in my vocabulary. I want it all, and 
not in a bunch of pretty-looking, half-page reports either. I want it 
all together, packed on one sheet that I can take into a meeting in 
case I need to look something up. And I need it every week,” pro-
claims Ari Kwon, vice president of Links Meats, a large, family-
owned sausage company.
During an interview, Kwon has been grilling Eun Lee, who is 
part of the systems analysis team that is busy designing an infor -
mation system for Links Meats. Although Eun is hesitant about 
what Kwon has told her, she proceeds to design a printed report 
that includes all the important items the team has settled on during 
the analysis phase.
When a prototype of the new report, designed to her specifica-
tions, is handed to Ari, however, there appears to be a change of 
heart. Kwon says in no uncertain terms that she can’t find what 
she needs.
3. Text
4. Graphics
5. Presentation style
6. Navigation
7. Promotion
Structure. Planning the structure of a website is one of the most important steps in developing a 
professional website. Think about your goals and objectives. Each page in the overall web struc-
ture should have a distinct message or other related information. Sometimes it is useful to examine 
professional sites to analyze them for content and features. Figure 11.16 is a screen capture from 
the DinoTech website. The purpose for the site and the web medium work well together. Notice 
that there is great attention to supporting users on the site. There are words, graphics, JPEG 
images, and icons. In addition, there are many kinds of links: to RSS feeds, video, subwebs, chat 
rooms, a search engine, and many other features.
To help plan and maintain a solid structure, a web developer can benefit from using one of 
the many website diagramming and mapping tools available. Many software packages have web 
charting options built into the software. Although helpful for development, these tools become 
even more important when maintaining a website. Given the dynamic nature of the web, sites 
that are linked to your site may move at any time, requiring you or your web developer to update 
the links. Look for applications that perform web functionality testing or website error checking.
Content. Provide something important to website users. Exciting animation, movies, and sound 
are fun, but you must include appropriate content to keep the user interested. Supply some timely 
advice, important information, a free offer, a top 10 list of tips on the page’s topic, or any activity 
that you can provide that is interactive and moves users away from a browsing mode and into an 
interactive one.
“Stickiness” is a quality a website can possess. If a user stays at your site for a long period 
of time, your site has a high degree of stickiness. That is why a merchant includes many items of 
interest on a site. An online travel company, for example, may post videos on how to successfully 
pack a carry-on bag for a weekend stay or a months-long trip to a foreign country.

## PAGE 380
344    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
Use a metaphor or images that provide a metaphor for your site. You can use a theme, such  
as a storefront, with additional pages having a variety of metaphors related to the storefront, 
such as a deli. Avoid the overuse of cartoons, and don’t be repetitive.
Every website should include an FAQ (frequently asked questions) page. Often these are 
created based on the experiences of users and technical support people who identify the topics of 
continuing concern. Eighty percent of the questions will fall into the FAQ category. By having 
answers readily available, 24 hours a day, you will save valuable employee time and also save 
user time. FAQ pages also demonstrate to users of your site that you are in concert with them and 
have a good idea of what they would like to know.
A website may take advantage of prewritten applications. Examples include search engines, 
mapping software, weather information, and news and stock tickers. Website designers value 
these packages because they can increase the functionality of the site, and the additional features 
encourage users to bookmark their clients’ websites because they provide valuable bonus content.
Text. Remember that text is important too. Each web page should have a title. Place meaningful 
words in the first sentence appearing on your web page. Let people know that they have indeed 
navigated to the right website. Clear writing is especially important.
Content on ecommerce sites needs to be constantly updated. Content management systems 
(CMSs) are powerful software tools that can enable the analyst to develop and maintain websites 
and other online applications. An increasingly popular CMS is Joomla!, which can be found 
at https://www.joomla.org. It is based on PHP and MySQL. Unlike proprietary CMSs, which 
are expensive and not widely available, Joomla! is an open source solution that is made freely 
available to any developer under a general public license (GPL).

The DinoTech website makes the most of links, RSS feeds, video subscriptions, and banner ads. (Used with permission from Microsoft)

Graphics. The following list provides details about creating effective graphics for websites:
1. Use one of the most commonly used image formats. PNGs and JPEGs are best for 
photographs, and GIFs are best for artwork images. GIFs are limited to 256 colors but may 
include a transparent background, pixels that allow the background to show through the 
GIF image. GIF images may also be interlaced, meaning that the web browser will show 
the image in successive stages, presenting a clearer image with each stage.
2. Keep the background simple and make sure users can read the text clearly. When 
using a background pattern, make sure that you can see the text clearly on top of it.
3. Create a few professional-looking graphics for use on your pages.
4. Keep graphic images small, and reuse bullets or navigational buttons. Commonly 
used images are stored in a cache, an area on the browsing computer’s hard drive. Once an 
image has been received, it will be taken from the cache whenever it is used again. Using 
cached images improves the speed with which a browser can load a web page.
5. Include tooltip text in a title attribute for image hot spots. Here is an example. If you 
display a map of the United States, each state could be set up as a hot spot. The name of 
the state would appear whenever the user moves the mouse pointer over an image of a 
state. Note that image spots are not as useful for mobile devices because there is no cursor 
that can hover over the image.
6. Examine your website on a variety of displays and screen resolutions as well as on 
smartphones and tablets. Scenes and text that look great on a high-end video display may 
not look good on other devices.
Presentation Style. The following list gives added details about how to design engaging entry 
displays for websites:
1. Provide a home page or landing page that introduces the visitor to the website. The 
page must be designed to load quickly. A useful rule of thumb is to design a page that 
will load in 3 seconds. (Although you may be designing the page on a workstation at the 
university, a visitor to your website may be accessing it from home, using a slower internet 
connection.) This entry display should be 100 kilobytes or less, including all graphics.
Blogs are being written by corporate users for both internal and 
external communication. Blogs are informal and personal, and they 
often invite comments and feedback. They are easy to create and 
update and are designed to change daily. Companies are using blogs 
for advertising and to build social networks for consumers, clients, 
and vendors around their products, building trust and customer 
relationships.
Corporate blogs are monitored out of a sense of responsibility 
for the participants. Guidelines, policies, and laws that shape moni-
toring practices include shared cultural, ethical, and legal values 
such as respecting other employees and customers; not publishing 
any sensitive or secret corporate information or anything protected 
by copyright (without permission); and excluding anything that is 
hateful or profane or that violates anyone’s privacy.
Even with all these guidelines, you still need to ensure that blog 
posts are written in a human voice, not immersed in legal language. 
The latest entry should be at the start of the blog. It should contain 
the following elements:
1. The permalink, or permanent link, specific for the blog post, 
which should never change
2. The headline or title of the post
3. The primary link, which connects the reader to the subject 
under discussion
4. An optional summary, often appearing after the link
5. The blog text or commentary
6. An optional image
7. A block quote containing quotations or other material  
from other sources that contributes to the discussion  
(often indented or in a different font to set it apart from  
the main text)
8. Links for comments by other people
9. Blog software features such as a calendar, search form, and 
other universal features
One highly acclaimed desktop blogging editor for the Mac is 
MarsEdit by Red Sweater. It is compatible with WordPress, 
Micro.blog, Tumblr, and many other blog publishing platforms.
MAC APPEAL  
CREATING BLOGS

## PAGE 382
346    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
The home page should contain a number of choices, much like a menu. An easy way to 
accomplish that is to design a set of links or buttons and position them on the left side or the top 
of the screen. These can be linked to other pages on the same website.
2. Keep the number of graphics to a reasonable minimum. It takes additional download 
time to transfer a graphics-intensive site.
3. Use large and colorful fonts for headings.
4. Use interesting images and buttons for links. A group of images combined into a single 
image is called an image map, and it contains various hot spots that act as links to other 
pages.
5. Use cascading style sheets to control the formatting and layout of the web page. A CSS 
separates the content (the text and images) from how it looks (the presentation) and stores 
the information in a file external to the web page. One style sheet may control the format-
ting of many pages. Making a change in the style sheet (for example, changing the color of 
bold text) will change the formatting of all the web pages that use the style sheet.
6. Use divisions to enhance a layout. Divisions control the layout by providing blocks of 
text on the web page. Each block may be defined with a position from the top and left of 
the screen or a larger block, and it may have a width and height, as well as border style and 
background color. Divisions eliminate the need for tables within tables and simplify design.
7. Use the same graphics images on several web pages. Consistency will be improved, and 
the pages will load more quickly because the computer stores the image in a cache and 
doesn’t have to load it again.
8. Use JavaScript. JavaScript can be used to tell images to change when a mouse is moved 
and hovers over them, making menus expand and so on. JavaScript can also be used to 
reformat a web page based on the height and width of the screen. If the website is multina-
tional, JavaScript can even detect the language being used (a browser setting) and redirect 
the viewer to a different landing page displaying text in a different language.
9. Avoid overusing animation, sound, and other elements.
Navigation. Is it fun for you to follow links on the web? The answer is most likely that it depends. 
When you discover a website that loads easily, has meaningful links, and allows you to easily 
return to the places you want to go back to, chances are you think it is fun. Fun is not just play; it 
can be an important part of work too. Recent research shows that fun can have a powerful effect 
on making computer training effective.
If, on the other hand, you can’t decide which button or hot spot to click on, and you are afraid 
to choose the wrong one because you might get into the wrong page that takes a long time to load, 
navigation is more painful than fun. An example is visiting a software company’s page to find 
information about the features of the latest version of a product. You have choices such as products, 
download, FAQ, and tech support. Which button will lead to the answers you’re looking for?
Make sure you include a navigation bar and links to the home page on every page of the 
website. A visitor to the site may have found a particular page using a search engine, and it is 
important that the visitor can easily find their way to the home page.
Most important, make sure that users can find what they want quickly: users should be able 
to move from the page they are currently on to the page containing the information they want 
using well-planned, meaningful menu items that provide a mix of cues (Budiu, 2020), including 
those they can get from a link label, the context in which the link is shown, and their previous 
experiences with websites. Use breadcrumbs to help orient the user. Additonally, designers should 
avoid menu labels with odd or branded names.
Rather than urging designers to follow the the three-click rule (that states users should be able 
to move from the page they are currently on to the page containing the information they want in 
only three clicks of a mouse button) to the point of absurdity, and trading off depth of the menu 
for the sake of observing a rigid rule, research (Nielsen & Li, 2017) recommends focusing on 
designing so that users are successful in finding the content they want. One approach is mega-
menus, which are large, two-dimensional panels you can divide into groups showing options for 
navigation.
These options can be further structured through layout, type font design, and even icons. 
Megamenus show everything visible all at one time, which means the user does not have to do 
any scrolling. Users can reveal menu options by hovering, clicking, or tapping on an icon or link.

## PAGE 383
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     347
In order to addess accessibility issues with megamenus, they recommend that designers make 
each top-level menu choice clickable, where it leads to a regular web page that can then present 
all available drop-down options in a fully accessible, HTML version. Examples of websites with 
megamenus for you to explore and use as prototypes include the insurance firm daveramsey.com 
and the fashion and home retailer johnlewis.com.
Promotion. Promote your site. Don’t assume that search engines will find you right away. Submit 
your site every few months to various search engines. Include keywords, called metatags, that 
search engines will use to link search requests to your site. You can find general information 
about metatags at searchenginewatch.com. In addition, you can download free metatag-generating 
software from a variety of sources. If you try to use email to promote your site, others will consider 
it junk email or spam.
Encourage your readers to bookmark your website. If you link to and suggest that they go 
to affiliated websites that feature the “best movie review page in the world” or to the “get music 
for free” website, don’t assume they’ll be coming back to your site in the near future. You will 
encourage them to revisit if they bookmark your site (bookmarks are called “favorites” in some 
browsers). You also may want to design a “favicon,” or favorite icon, so users can identify your 
site in their lists of favorites.
WEB 2.0 TECHNOLOGIES
Of increasing importance for an analyst working on organizational websites is the strategic inclu-
sion of Web 2.0 technologies that focus on enabling and facilitating user-generated content and 
collaboration via the web. Familiar types of technologies you should think about including on both 
public-facing and internal websites you design include blogs, wikis, links to social networks on 
which the company has a presence, and tagging (also called social bookmarking) that provides 
useful pointers to online resources such as websites, content on corporate intranets, corporate 
Seymour continues, “I can tell you what we disliked about the 
reports I worked with. There were too many darn layers, too much 
foliage, or whatever you call it, to go through. Even with a screen 
in front of me, it was like paging through a thick report. What do 
you call that?”
“Menus?” Rosa suggests helpfully. “The main point is that you 
didn’t like having to go through lots of information to get to the 
display you needed.”
Seymour looks happily at Rosa and says, “You’ve got it. I want 
to see more fields on each screen.”
How should Rosa design screen output so that Fields and the 
VP group can get what they want on each screen while observing 
the guidelines for good display design? Remember that the 
group members are busy, and although they wouldn’t be caught 
without their smartphones, they don’t use their desktop systems 
very often. Design a hyperlinked page that would work well in a 
DSS for the vice presidents. What should be included in the first 
display, and what should be stored in hyperlinks? List elements 
for each and explain in a paragraph why you have decided on 
this strategy.
CONSULTING OPPORTUNITY 11.5 
A Field Day
“The thing of it is, I get impatient,” says Seymour Fields, 
owner of a chain of 15 highly successful florist shops/indoor flo-
ral markets called Fields that are located in three Midwestern cit-
ies. “See this thing here?” He taps his computer display irritatedly. 
“We do all the payroll and all the accounting with these things, but  
I don’t use it like I should. I actually feel a little guilty about it. 
See?” he says as he makes a streak on the display with his finger. 
“It’s even got dust on it. I’m a practical person, though. If it’s sitting 
here, taking up space, I want to use it. Or smell it, or at least enjoy 
looking at it, like flowers, right? Or weed it out, that’s what I say. 
The one time I tried something with it, it was a real disaster. Well, 
look, I can show you if I still remember how.” Seymour proceeds to 
try to open an application but can’t seem to get it working.
Rosa Maceta, a systems analyst, has been working on a systems 
project for the entire Fields chain. Part of the original proposal was 
to provide Seymour and their vice presidents with a decision support 
system (DSS) that would help them devise a strategy to determine 
which European markets to visit to set up purchase agreements for 
fresh flowers, which outlets to ship particular kinds of flowers to, 
and how much general merchandise, such as planters, vases, note 
cards, and knickknacks, to stock in each outlet.

## PAGE 384
348    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
documents, or photos that are relevant to the organization and to users. Notice that Web 2.0 tech-
nologies can be externally faced to clients, customers, vendors, or suppliers or internally faced 
to corporate intranets.
For outward-facing web technologies, the reasons for adding these important collaborative 
tools to organization websites are clear. Companies use collaborative tools to communicate an 
integrated branding and messaging strategy across multiple platforms, to gauge consumer opinion, 
to gather feedback, to create a community of users, and so on. Inward-facing web technologies 
can be useful in building employee relationships, maintaining trust, sharing knowledge, innovating 
among employees and groups of employees, locating corporate resources more readily, and 
nurturing corporate culture and subcultures inside the organization.
Your requirements determination phase informed you about user preferences. As you are 
prototyping, you might also want to add Web 2.0 technologies to internal and external web pages 
if there is a well-thought-out, strategic reason for doing so. Part of your recommended design 
solutions may include adding collaborative tools. From our research and practice with for-profit 
and nonprofit organizations attempting to use Web 2.0 technologies, we have found that there are 
five aspects an analyst should consider:
1. Realize differences between corporate objectives and objectives of key stakeholders. 
There will be differences in why each group values collaborative technologies that you dis-
cover in the outlooks of both groups.
2. Serve as the voice of the customer to your client organization. In recommending the 
inclusion of Web 2.0 technologies for externally facing websites, you need to be able to 
articulate the needs of the customer to the organization.
3. Recognize the importance of visual page design for effectively displaying collaborative 
tools. Whenever users have an expectation of placement of Web 2.0 icons (see Figure 11.17 
for examples) on the web page (for Facebook links, Twitter icons, tagging capability, and 
so forth), you must observe the convention, or at least reinforce the emerging convention. 
Users are getting more accustomed to seeing this array of interactive options displayed in a 
predictable pattern on corporate websites.
4. Revise and update the Web 2.0 technologies offered frequently. Develop a plan (and a  
set of tools) for revising and updating collaborative tools offered on both internal and external 
websites when styles, customs, and tools change.
5. Work to integrate Web 2.0 technologies with the existing branding. Ensure that 
messages are consistent throughout outwardly facing websites and in all public-facing 
communications.

of icons. (Courtesy: Facebook,

SOCIAL MEDIA DESIGN
Designing output and content for social media has become a career in its own right, but systems 
analysts may be faced with those design tasks as part of their overall information systems design 
work for an organization. Businesses use social media for a number of reasons, including growing 
their audiences (by targeting users within their key demographics), increasing traffic to already 
well-established websites, reinforcing brand awareness, developing an online personal website that 
can be trusted, and so on. Many fun, interactive activities can be added to social media posts such 
as games, contests, competitions, rewards, or point or badge prizes that engage, incentivize, and 
reward users for taking an action (going through their Pinterest account to pin a shirt purchased 
from Amazon to Pinterest, for example).
Your clients have numerous options to consider for posting content or advertisements that can 
make an impact on customers: Facebook, Instagram (via the company’s Facebook Advertising), 
Twitter (your client will need to sign up for Twitter Advertising), Snapchat, YouTube, LinkedIn, 
Pinterest, and many others based in other countries. Although social platforms share some simi-
larities, they also have some differences. On Snapchat, for instance, videos and photos disappear 
once they are viewed, whereas Twitter reuses the main profile picture and username every time 
the Twitter account is accessed. Carefully craft initial selections for these sites, and refresh with 
new pictures or other images as warranted.
Designing content for social media is highly visual, and visual content is 40 times more likely 
to be shared on social media. Sharing is at the core of what allows a designer of social media to 
leverage social media for its best networking aspects. Although designing for social media is built 
on many of the same principles as designing for intranet business pages or for corporate web-
sites, the emphasis shifts from clarity of writing and orderly design to an emphasis on the visual. 
Specialized imaging tools such as Canva, Pablo by Buffer, Adobe Creative Cloud Express, and 
Desygner are easy to use and enhance visual design of social media content.
Guidelines for Social Media Design
In the following section we consider four aspects of designing for social media: (1) emphasizing 
the goal of the design, (2) developing a consistent look, (3) creating appealing design flow, and 
(4) simplifying to use space in a positive way.
Emphasizing the Goal of the Design. Always keep in mind the most important goal of the con-
tent to be conveyed. Is it to invite participation? Is it to engage first-time customers with a new 
company? Is it to remind an established customer of upcoming events? Is it to underscore the 
presence of the corporation on social media in addition to their web presence? Examine what is 
driving creation of this content. Keep focusing on what must be included and eliminate second- 
and third-priority social media content, which should be developed on more extended platforms 
such as websites or in videos.
Developing a Consistent Look. Incorporate key design elements such as corporate logos or 
trademarks in every posting on social media. Continue using the organization’s established color 
palette (for example, use green, brown, and blue for an organic food company as visual cues of 
the freshness of organic food grown outdoors), and carefully consider the story you tell in your 
designs for a company that provides luxury services or goods (black and gold are traditional 
invocations of lavish goods and would be good color choices).
Color choice can help the audience understand the story you are trying to tell; critical elements 
in the story can be keyed to certain colors to increase their impact on the audience. Appropriate 
color choices can be found using design tools with predesigned complementary palletes or by 
examining a color wheel.
When beginning a new design, make sure the color choices reflect the organization’s purpose 
and harmonize with their corporate logo. As with other types of screen and form design, limit the  
number of typefaces on postings to about three. Using too many typefaces risks distracting 
the audience with clutter and losing the story line.
Creating Appealing Design Flow. Lay out the design in a deliberate way so that it flows well when 
the user views it. The upper left side of the screen and the left side are highly noticeable places 
for important information to appear on social media. For those familiar with the English alphabet,

## PAGE 386
350    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
visualize elements for the design as being mapped out in an “E” pattern, an “F” pattern, or a “Z” 
pattern. In reading English text, our eyes follow those patterns, so key visual elements are best 
placed there. These layouts work well for social media and will garner attention for the business, 
keeping people on the posting or advertisement longer than will other designs.
Simplifying to Use Space in a Positive Way. Understanding the concept of simplifying designs 
for social media is sometimes a difficult one to convey because it dwells on the idea that “less is 
more.” Positive space is like a “rest” in a musical score. A rest is a silent pause in the music, and 
the score symbolically shows how long the musician must pause before the music resumes. A rest 
contrasts with the sound of the note, but it is crucial to a coherent sound. A useful way to approach 
using positive space is to design a content posting, then ask yourself “What can be eliminated?” 
Oftentimes you can delete an extraneous element—an extra object, an extra color—or resize an 
element and use the positive space to set off important objects in an eye-catching way.
Eventually, one goal in developing a strategy for addressing evolving user standards and 
conventions is to use text analytics (TA) software to interpret the qualitative data captured in 
customer responses to blogs, wikis, and through other social media. Indeed, this is an approach you 
can recommend to your client organization as you close the feedback loop created by establishing 
Web 2.0 technologies for consumers and employees.
DESIGNING APPS FOR SMARTPHONES AND TABLETS
As smartphones and tablets become more powerful and ubiquitous in organizations, systems ana-
lysts will need to conceptualize their software as apps. In the early days of computing, software 
was called a “program.” Apple preferred the term “application.” When software was designed for 
the iPhone and iPad, software was simply called an “app.” The word app became mainstream 
with the widespread adoption of iPhones and iPads that can run these small programs. Apple sells 
apps on iTunes much the same way it sells music. You can download an app and install it on your 
iPhone or iPad.
Creating an app for a mobile phone or a larger device such as the iPad involves brainstorming, 
imagining, preliminary screen design, user interface decisions, and detailed screen design. Many 
designers like to work in Adobe Illustrator, but others prefer Adobe Photoshop. But before 
attempting to use one of these packages, a large whiteboard with a marker can be the very best 
way to begin designing an app. Later in this chapter we examine apps that are used to create 
mockups, the preferred name for smartphone and tablet prototypes.
An analyst who develops apps is trying to fill or to create a user need in a unique way. The 
motivation for creating the app often arises from the developer, and usually not from formal 
requirements analysis performed for an organization. However, numerous approaches and tools 
used for display and web design apply to app development as well as to larger systems projects.
In this section, we focus on designing for the small screens of smartphones and tablets. It is 
interesting to note, however, that Apple requires developers to send in a 512 × 512-pixel icon for 
the App Store. Requirements for the original Macintosh computer only necessitated a 512 × 342-
pixel display, so output design for smartphones and tablets must be taken seriously.
Throughout this section, we discuss concepts useful in any operating system, whether for 
Apple, Android, or Microsoft. We use examples from Apple’s iPad and iPhone, however, because 
they are currently the dominant devices of tablets and smartphones.
In designing apps for the small screens of smartphones and tablets, follow these steps:
1. Set up a developer account.
2. Choose a development process.
3. Be original.
4. Determine how you will price the app.
5. Follow the rules for output design.
6. Design your icon.
7. Choose an appropriate name for the app.
8. Design for a variety of devices.
9. Design the output for the app.
10. Design the output a second time for different orientation.

## PAGE 387
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     351
11. Share a prototype of your work.
12. Design the logic.
13. Design movement.
14. Create the user interface using gestures.
15. Protect your intellectual property.
16. Market your app.
We concentrate on the steps that are design oriented in this chapter.
Set Up a Developer Account
Apple has software developer accounts for individuals or an enterprise. Apple insists on approving 
every app sold in its App Store. This means there are some restrictions that frustrate software 
developers and, sometimes, consumers. Here’s where the walled garden comes in (in this case 
the metaphor refers to an array of preapproved apps that are closed to outside developers). You 
won’t find any app with objectionable content in the App Store, and you won’t find a great app 
that lets you reprogram the mute button on your iPhone to function as a camera shutter either. 
Android has a more open platform, and many developers have taken advantage of developing apps 
in open source communities.
Choose a Development Process
Unless you are developing a specialized app that will cost more than $19.95, prototyping is most 
likely the best way to develop your app. Quick releases are important, and there is a strong advan-
tage to being the first to introduce a certain type of app. You can draw on many of the principles 
and values of agile development in designing an app.
Quality should not be sacrificed, but it is possible to introduce an app and then add features 
in subsequent releases. Numerous app developers have adopted this strategy, and it has a number 
of advantages. It allows you to gain an advantage by being the first to introduce a specific app. It 
also allows you to revise the app adding new features, and users approve wholeheartedly when 
improvements are made. In addition, it increases visibility because the app appears on a list of 
apps that have been updated.
Be Original
Never copy other developers’ software. Doing so is illegal and unethical. Make sure your app 
has different functionality and looks very different from other apps on the market. Observe all 
licensing restrictions.
This may seem obvious, but some developers don’t realize that you can’t use Apple trade-
marks in your app. You should avoid using words like iPad  and iPhone  in your app, and even 
avoid the name Apple, for that matter.
Determine How You Will Price the App
The pricing decision you make will affect the design of the app. If your app will contain adver -
tising, the ads will take up valuable space on your screen and may distract users. If you adopt a 
pricing strategy that allows upgrading of features, you need to consider the interface that asks the 
user to upgrade.
There are six basic options for pricing:
1. Choose a low-cost strategy. Taking into consideration free and freemium apps and apps 
offered in the Apple App Store, the average price of apps is around $1.00 and the average 
price of games is $0.49. A very large number of iPhone apps are $0.99. For the iPad, $1.99 
rather than $0.99 is the standard. If you decide to offer a low-cost app, you’ll have plenty 
of company.
2. Introduce an app as a “premium” app. Premium apps cost $19.95 and up. These apps 
are intended for professionals and are often iPad versions of desktop software. Omni 
Group offers a suite of apps that accomplish much of the functionality of Mac apps. If 
you price your app this way, you will have fewer users adopting the app, but they will 
be serious users. They will demand that you add features and functionality and provide 
adequate support.

## PAGE 388
352    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
The Hot Apps Factory team introduced an app for developing apps as version .983 
with an introductory price of $19.95. They are planning to add six additional features and 
incrementally increase the price by $5.00 every time a feature is added. Version 2.0 will be 
priced as a monthly subscription for $10-$200 a month depending on chosen features. 
3. Adopt a “freemium” model. Apps that are offered at no cost for basic features but allow 
in-app purchases for premium features and functionality are called freemium apps. In 
the early days of app development, there was somewhat of a backlash to this approach, 
as many users feel they were forced to pay for each feature separately, but now it is more 
common. Consider all options before choosing this business model.
4. Offer an app for free. Sometimes it is advisable to offer an app at no charge to the user. 
If you are the nth person to write an app for this situation, that may be the only way to get 
users to adopt your app. It may be useful if you have other apps for sale. Adopters may 
appreciate your work and buy something else you have developed.
5. Promote an app by reducing its price. A mixed strategy is sometimes best. Occasionally 
you can reduce the price of an app for a short period of time or even offer it for free. Apple 
iTunes features an app of the week, although it is not free. However, other sites feature 
daily free offerings. Ideally you want to be on one of Apple’s top lists and offering an app 
for free might accomplish this.
6. Accept advertising. If you have a very useful app that is open much of the time (a weather 
app, a stock market app, or even a popular game, for example), it might be advantageous to 
have a free app with advertising. If you read the comments of users about a certain weather 
app on the App Store pages, you will notice that customers keep asking for an ad-free 
option. Use advertising cautiously.
Follow the Rules
Follow the design rules set forth by the developer of the operating system. Apple rejects many apps 
designed for Apple’s iOS because they violate the Human Interface Guidelines (HIG). Interfaces 
are discussed in detail in Chapter 14.
Design Your Icon
On a smartphone or tablet, the icon is just as important as the app name. Try to design a simple, 
easily identifiable, and easy-to-remember icon. Figure 11.18 shows an example from App Cooker, 
developed by Hot Apps Factory. It allows you to see how your icon will look in various different 
formats.
FIGURE 11.18
App Cooker (by Hot Apps 
Factory) allows a designer to 
see how an app icon will appear 
in its many forms. (Screenshot 
from App Cooker application.

## PAGE 389
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     353
Choose an Appropriate Name for the App
Keep your app name to 12 or fewer characters. It will appear under the icon on the home screen, 
and if it is longer than 12 letters, some letters will be dropped when the icon is displayed on 
a screen.
Design for a Variety of Devices
Even staying within Apple’s set of iPads and iPhones may mean that you need to design apps 
that are flexible. Some of the differences among displays are shown in Figure 11.19. You need to 
supply Apple with a number of different icon sizes, so take that into consideration when you are 
developing an icon. Icons will look different from one device to another if they are too complex. 
Simplicity is a virtue when designing icons.
You should create icons in a vector-based graphic application such as Adobe Illustrator. If 
you use vector-based graphics, you can scale your icons rather than create bitmap images in many 
different sizes. Start with a 1024 × 1024 design, and you can shrink the graphic to smaller sizes. 
If you create a 1024 × 1024 design, it will print as a 3.5 × 3.5-inch item at the 300 dots per inch 
resolution needed for magazines.
Design the Output for the App
Although it is possible to use Adobe Illustrator, Visio (on the PC), or OmniGraffle (on a Mac), you 
might consider using one of the many apps designed specifically for creating apps on handheld 
devices. Some apps especially useful for designing for Apple iPhones and iPads are AppCraftHD, 
iMockups, and App Cooker.
Figure 11.20 shows how the App Cooker app allows a designer to pick from a series of wid-
gets. This app, as well as many others, provides templates for iPhones and iPads so a designer 
can plan precisely what will appear on the screen when the app is running. The principles for 
designing smartphone and tablet screens are similar to the ones mentioned earlier in the chapter.
Design the Output a Second Time for a Different Orientation
Once you have designed all the screens for an app, you should do it again, but this time turn the 
devices 90 degrees. If you designed the app in landscape mode, turn the device and design it in 
portrait mode.

Let’s face it. Some apps look better in one mode than another. If you read a full page of text 
on a smartphone in a browser such as Safari, you may be familiar with looking at it in portrait 
mode. On a tablet, however, the text may appear to look better in landscape mode, even if the 
number of words is exactly the same.
When text is justified, the gaps between words seem to be larger in portrait mode, making it 
more difficult to read. On a device such as Kindle, changing the orientation changes the num-
ber of words on a page and also changes the column structure. Users like to choose their screen 
orientation.
Developers can use different orientations to their advantage. In portrait mode, a calculator 
app can present a very simple calculator, but putting it in landscape mode may transform it into a 
powerful scientific calculator. Giving this easy option to a customer is usually welcomed.
Share a Prototype of Your Work
Before you begin coding, take the opportunity to get valuable feedback from friends, potential 
users, and even fellow developers. Apps such as AppTaster allow you to test your app designs by 
sharing your prototypes created in App Cooker.
Design the App’s Logic
Tablets and smartphones fit in well with the prototyping method of development, but sometimes 
the best way to approach an app is to sketch out the logic using some of the principles used in 
creating process specifications and diagramming structured decisions (discussed in Chapter 9).
Design Movement
How you move from screen to screen may make a big difference in a user’s experience. Don’t 
leave movement to chance. Plan movement carefully and use storyboards or an app such as App 
Cooker to plan how an app navigates from one screen to another, as shown in Figure 11.21.
Create the User Interface Using Gestures
Smartphones and tablets have innovative user interfaces (technically called touchscreen capaci-
tive sensing), and you need to design your apps assuming that users will demand touch-sensitive 
interfaces that use gestures such as swipes, pinches, tugs, and shakes. Users will expect different 
types of feedback and will demand the ability to opt out of some of the features.
Gestures and feedback for tablets and smartphones are discussed in greater detail in 
Chapter 14, in the context of human–computer interaction, providing user feedback, and usability.
FIGURE 11.20
The mockup app App Cooker has 
a toolbox of widgets you can use 
to design a screen. (From App 
Cooker application. Copyright © 
2011 by Hot Apps Factory SARL. 
Reprinted with permission.)

## PAGE 391
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     355
Protect Your Intellectual Property
If you put the effort into design, you need to protect it. Trademark your icons and logos. Recently 
competitors have introduced apps with one-off logos or icons very similar to already popular ones. 
You might also want to copyright your app. Doing so is not very expensive.
You are also advised to create your own end user license agreement (EULA). Your EULA 
gives others the right to use your app and limits what a user can do with the app.
When you write a EULA, consider including a warranty disclaimer. Some users will not like 
an app and will want a refund. This is often unreasonable, especially when a user may have paid 
only 99 cents to buy the app. Still, users’ expectations are not always aligned with yours, so protect 
yourself. Apple has a default EULA, but our advice is to consider a custom EULA.
The smartphone and tablet markets are constantly evolving. New operating systems will 
change the way apps are designed in the future.
Market Your App
If you are fortunate enough to get a potential user to visit your app’s product page, you need to 
convince the person to pay for and download your app. Pay attention to the content on Apple’s 
App Store, which is divided into many sections. To market your app, you will need a large icon, 
a description, a section explaining what is new in the current version, and a sample set of screen 
shots. The screen shots selected are very important. All this is mentioned here because good app 
design is essential to putting your best face forward.
There are many other ways to market your app, but that discussion is beyond the scope of 
this book. There are many books and websites devoted to marketing apps.
OUTPUT PRODUCTION AND XML
Output production varies depending on the platform used. There are many different ways to 
create output, ranging from database software such as Microsoft Access, to statistical packages 
such as SAS, to document creators such as Adobe Acrobat. Figure 11.22 illustrates the 
transformation process.
One of the advantages of using hypertext markup language (XML; see Chapter 8) is that an 
XML document can be transformed into different output media types. This is done using CSSs or 
extensible stylesheet language transformations (XSLT). These methods reinforce the idea that 
data can be defined once and used many times in different formats.
FIGURE 11.21
App Cooker allows you to plan 
how an app goes from one screen 
to the next. (From App Cooker 
application. Copyright © 2011 
by Hot Apps Factory SARL. 
Reprinted with permission.)

## PAGE 392
356    PART iv •  THE EssEnTiALs  Of  DEsign
Using CSSs is an easy way to transform an XML document. A style sheet provides a series 
of styles, such as font family, size, color, border, and so on, that are linked to the elements of the 
XML document. These styles may vary for different media, such as a screen, printed output, or 
a handheld device. The transforming software detects the type of device and applies the correct 
styles to control the output.
For example, a style used for a flat-panel display might use a rich palette of colors and a sans 
serif font, which is easier to read on a screen. A different style using a serif font and black or gray 
color may be used to define a printed report for the same data. A smaller font size might be used 
for a handheld device or mobile phone.
The drawback of using CSSs is that it does not allow an analyst to manipulate the data, such 
as rearranging the order of the elements or sorting, and only a limited amount of identifying text, 
such as captions, may be added. CSS is basically used for formatting.
Extensible stylesheet language transformations (XSLT) is a more powerful means of trans-
forming an XML document. XSLT allows an analyst to select the elements and insert them into 
a web page or another output medium. XSLT is not a programming language but uses a series of 
statements to define which elements should be output, the sort sequence, the selection of data, 
and so on. An example of an XML transformation is illustrated in Figure 11.23. The XML is 
shown on the left, and the result of the transformation is shown on the right. Notice that only the 
data between the tags (contained in less than [<] and greater than [>] symbols) are included in 
the output.
Ajax
Ajax is a technique that uses both JavaScript and XML to obtain small amounts of data, either 
plain text or XML, from a server without leaving the web page. This is a big advantage because 
it means that the entire web page does not need to be reloaded. It works by allowing the web 
page to reformat itself based on choices that a user inputs. Ajax is also related to user input; see 
additional details in Chapter 12.
Ajax is discussed here because the output implications are important as well. It is up to 
analysts and designers to determine when data should be added or changed on a web page and 
to identify the conditions that cause the change. The order in which the questions are asked plays 
into this design as well.
FIGURE 11.22
Extensible stylesheet language 
transformations software can be 
used to make XML documents and 
transform them into many different 
formats for a variety of platforms.

## PAGE 393
CHAPTER 11  •  DEsigning EffECTivE  OuTPuT     357
An example of a web page using Ajax is shown in Figure 11.24, which demonstrates that Ajax 
makes it possible to display much less data on a page, thereby making the output less cluttered 
and less confusing. In this example, the user entered one of four ways to narrow down the search 
to view a list of current customers. The options the user had available were (1) enter the first three 
digits of a zip code (postal code), (2) enter a telephone area code, (3) select a state, or (4) select 
a country. The user may not know the postal code or area code and may therefore need to search 
by state or country, so the options are very useful.
After entering one of the location choices—in this case, the first three digits of the postal 
code—the user clicked on the Get Customers button. The value of the postal code is sent to the 
server, along with data indicating that it was a postal code. The server then finds all customer 
records for the selected location, creates an XML document, and sends it to the same web page.
When designing output, a systems analyst has many different options regarding how to 
display data on the web page. In this case, the analyst specified that the XML document would be 
used to create a drop-down list containing all current customers for the desired location. Once a 
user selects a customer from the drop-down list, more information about the particular customer 
is displayed, as shown in the example.
The advantage of using Ajax for displaying data is that the user does not have to wait for 
a new web page to display after making a selection. The Ajax philosophy is to display limited 
questions for the user to answer on an incremental basis. This eliminates screen clutter. Once the 
user responds to a question by making a choice, a new question may be generated.

Summary
Output is any useful information or data delivered to the user by the information system, decision support 
system, the web, or smartphones or tablets. Output can take virtually any form, including print, computer, 
smartphone, and tablet displays, audio, microforms, social media posts and content, and web-based documents.
A systems analyst has six main objectives in designing output: to serve the intended human and organi-
zation purpose, to fit the user, to deliver the right quantity of output, to deliver it to the right place, to provide 
output on time, and to choose the right output method.
Analysts should recognize that output content is related to output method. Output of different technolo-
gies affects users in different ways. Output technologies also differ in their speed, cost, portability, flexibility, 
accessibility, storage, and retrieval possibilities. All these factors must be considered when deciding among 
output options.
The presentation of output can bias users in their interpretation of it. Analysts and users must be 
aware of the sources of bias. Analysts should interact with users to design and customize output, inform users 
of the possibilities of bias in output, create flexible and modifiable output, and train users to use multiple 
outputs to help verify the accuracy of any particular report.
Designing output for user displays is important, especially for dashboards, decision support systems, 
the web, and smartphones and tablets. Responsive web design means that a website is developed so that it 
can be viewed on any device: a desktop, tablet, or smartphone. Recently designers have moved away from 
3-D, realistic designs (skeuomorphic design) to flat web design, which is a clean, two-dimensional design 
using bright colors.
Data visualization is the representation of data in the form of a chart, diagram, map, or other visual. It 
is used to make complex data understandable. For many people, seeing data displayed in a chart or map is 
easier to comprehend than if the same data were displayed in a table. Commercial data visualization packages 
are available. Some advantages of using data visualization are using viewing data in a better way, identifying 
trends and patterns, seeing more than one attribute at a time, quickly making decisions, and communicating 
information to others in a more effective manner.
Web 2.0 technologies (including social media) should be incorporated in both public-facing and internal 
web pages to permit user communication and collaboration. The analyst has several goals in designing social 
media output and content. They include emphasizing the goal of the design, developing a consistent look, 
creating an appealing design flow, and simplifying the design to use space in a positive way.
Aesthetics and usefulness are critical when creating well-designed output for displays. It is important 
to produce prototypes of screens and web documents that encourage users to interact with them and make 
changes where desired.
Developing applications, or “apps,” that are fun or useful for smartphones and tablets has its own distinct 
development process, but many aspects of agile development, such as quick releases or limited features, 
as well as prototyping, can be extended to app development. App designers who design apps for market-
dominant Apple smartphones and tablets should consider pricing options for apps, developing apps for 
FIGURE 11.24

the intellectual property they create.
Review Questions
1. List six objectives an analyst pursues in designing system output.
2. Contrast external outputs with internal outputs produced by a system. Remember to consider the 
differences between external and internal users.
3. List potential electronic output methods for users.
4. What are the drawbacks of electronic and web-based output?
5. List 10 factors that must be considered when choosing output technology.
6. What output type is best if frequent updates are necessary?
7. What kind of output is desirable if many readers will be reading, storing, and reviewing output over a 
period of years?
8. What are two of the drawbacks of audio output?
9. List three main ways in which presentations of output are unintentionally biased.
10. What are five ways an analyst can avoid biasing output?
11. Why is it important to show users a prototype output report or display?
12. List three categories of printed reports.
13. Give one difference between exception and summary reports.
14. In what ways do displays, printed output, and web-based documents differ?
15. List four guidelines to facilitate the design of good display output.
16. What differentiates output for a decision support system from that of a more traditional management 
information system that sends predetermined reports to decision makers?
17. What are the four primary considerations of an analyst in designing graphical output for a DSS?
18. Define stickiness.
19. List seven guidelines for creating good websites.
20. Define the term responsive web design.
21. Compare the difference between skeuomorphic design and flat web design.
22. List five guidelines for using graphics in designing websites.
23. List seven ideas for improving the presentation of corporate websites that you design.
24. What is the “three-clicks” rule? How stringently should it be followed?
25. What are megamenus for website navigation?
26. In what ways can you encourage companies to promote their websites that you have developed?
27. What is data visualization?
28. What are five of the many advantages for using data visualizations?
29. What are four “dos” for using visualization in your output designs?
30. What are four “don’ts” for using visualization in your output designs?
31. What are Web 2.0 technologies? List four of the many in existence.

Problems
1. “I’m sure they won’t mind if we start sending them the report on these oversized computer sheets. All 
this time, we’ve been condensing it, retyping it, and sending it to our biggest accounts, but we just 
can’t now. We’re so understaffed, we don’t have the time,” says Ada Aliyu. “I’ll just write a comment 
here telling them how to respond to this report, and then we can send it out.”
a. What potential problems do you see in casually changing external output? List them.
b. Discuss in a paragraph how internal and external output can differ in appearance and function.
2. “I don’t need to see it very often, but when I do, I have to be able to get at it quickly. I think we 
lost the last contract because the information I needed was buried in a stack of paper on someone’s 
desk somewhere,” says Hadiza Hussein, an architect describing the company’s problems to one of 
the analysts assigned to the new systems project. “What I need is instant information about how 
much a building of that square footage cost the last time we bid it; what the basic materials such as 
steel, glass, and concrete now cost from our three top suppliers; who our likely competition on this 
type of building might be; and who comprises the committee that will be making the final decision 
on who gets the bid. Right now, though, it’s in a hundred reports somewhere. I have to look all over 
for it.”
a. Given the limited details you have here, write a paragraph to suggest an output method for Hadi-
za’s use that will solve some of her current problems. In a second paragraph, explain your reasons 
for choosing the output method you did. (Hint: Be sure to relate output method to output content 
in your answer.)
b. Hadiza’s current thinking is that no paper record of the output discussed need be kept. In a para-
graph, discuss what factors should be weighed before displayed output is used to the exclusion of 
printed reports.
c. Make a list of five to seven questions concerning the output’s function in the organization that you 
would ask Hadiza and others before deciding to do away with any printed reports currently being 
used.
3. Here are several situations calling for decisions about output content, output methodology, distribu-
tion, and so on. For each situation, note the appropriate output decision.
a. A large, well-regarded supplier of key raw materials to your company’s production process 
requires a year-end summary report of totals purchased from it.
b. Internal brainstorming memos are circulated through the staff regarding plans for a company 
picnic and fundraiser.
c. A summary report of the company’s financial situation is needed by a key decision maker, who 
will use it when presenting a proposal to potential external backers.
d. A listing of the current night’s hotel room reservations is needed for front desk personnel.
e. A listing of the current night’s hotel room reservations is needed by the local police.
f. A real-time count of people passing through the gates of Wallaby World (an Australian theme 
park) will be used by parking lot patrols.
g. An inventory system must register an item each time it has been scanned by a wand.
h. A summary report of merit pay increases allotted to each of 120 employees will be used by  
22 supervisors during a joint supervisors’ meeting, and subsequently when explaining merit  
pay increases to the supervisors’ own department employees.
