422    PART iV  •  THE EssEnTiALs  OF DEsign
BUSINESS INTELLIGENCE
Although not a new concept, business intelligence (BI) has grown in prominence since the late 
1980s, and its uses have spread to several types of organizational employees, not just those few at 
the strategic level of decision making. Business intelligence is at the heart of a decision support 
system (DSS) for organizational decision makers. It is composed of features that gather and store 
data and uses knowledge management approaches combined with analysis. This becomes input 
to decision makers’ decision-making processes.
Business intelligence is built around the idea of processing large volumes of data. As a 
systems analyst, you may be asked to create systems that support BI, such as a data warehouse, 
the data from which is considered as the input to analytics. Or you might be involved with the 
creation of dashboards or even spreadsheets that convey BI to users. When data sets become too 
large or too complex to be handled with traditional tools or within traditional databases or data 
warehouses, they are often referred to as “big data.”
Demand for experts possessing information management skills is ever increasing because the 
growth in data far outstrips our abilities to meaningfully store, process, or analyze it. Big data also 
has been called an approach to design for companies, essentially a strategy that permits organiza-
tions to cope with ever-increasing amounts of data from myriad sources—some human-generated 
but many more generated via sensors of some type (for example, electronic road toll collections, 
satellite weather monitors).
Often analysts are in charge of the interface between users and a data warehouse. As with 
other systems, it is important to understand the data generated by the organization, the business 
itself, and the information requirements of the analysts who will be interpreting the output of any 
statistical models supported by the DSS.
Five prominent methods are used for analyzing business intelligence: slice-and-dice drill-
down, ad hoc queries, real-time analysis, forecasting, and scenarios. Although there has been 
some consolidation in the industry, there are many large and small providers of BI software. Some 
are moving their services to the cloud to provide on-demand services, reduce overall costs, and 
improve startup times.
Business analytics (BA) is a term that covers the ideas of using big data along with a variety 
of quantitatively based analytical tools (such as statistics and predictive modeling) to answer 
management questions about trends and what-if questions that analyze a number of actions as 
part of strategic scenarios. Their output may be used as input for decision makers or as input for 
computerized systems.
Some of the problems inherent in BI are how systems handle semistructured and unstructured 
data. Most organizations still have many documents that are not available for analysis through 
BI because they don’t fit the structured, stringent requirements of data warehouse entries. This 
means that a large portion of the important information and data an organization and its customers 
generate goes unanalyzed and, therefore, unused or underused in decision making. One approach 
to making the best use of this information is called data analytics.
DATA ANALYTICS
Data analytics use powerful algorithms to analyze vast amounts of structured data found in 
databases or to analyze huge quantities of unstructured real-time data that can support decision 
makers in making fact-based decisions. Analytics can refer to solving business problems and the 
decision-making process as well as the analytical approaches that help organizations create value 
through collecting, analyzing, and distributing large volumes of data. A broader term for both is 
business intelligence, or BI.
To understand the role the systems analyst plays in the use of data analytics (both the process 
and the tools), we consider eight aspects of this type of analysis:
1. Ensure the quality of the data.
2. Facilitate communication between users and data analytics professionals.
3. Educate users and decision makers about how to use analytical tools and how to interpret 
reports based on use of those tools.
4. Create reports that can be user generated.
5. Work with users who prefer to have a data analytics professional create reports.

## PAGE 459
CHAPTER 13  •  DEsigning D ATAb A sEs     423
6. Develop a collaborative business intelligence platform by collaborating on the creation of 
reports and making it possible for decision makers to share insights.
7. Ensure that the process of data analytics has the desired impact on a business.
8. Provide deep insights and understanding that supports decision makers and data analysts in 
making sense of their individual and organizational experience.
Any or all these eight actions may fall within the scope of the responsibilities of a systems 
analyst. Observe that many of these activities place the analyst as a collaborative partner with 
their client, and perhaps other data analysts. Ensuring the quality of the data to be analyzed is of 
utmost importance, as is a user-centered focus for any data analytics work done.
There are numerous ethical, privacy, and security considerations in using data generated in 
large, nonconventional databases. These databases include big data generated by people who 
participate on a routine or almost daily basis in social networks, blogs of social influencers, 
cloud applications for the public cloud, and public and government-sponsored data. Data 
are generated by people interacting with such systems who are largely unaware that the data are 
being used for data analytics purposes. A developer may use mashups of big data, combining 
Google maps and data from social media to develop networks, relationships, or predictions 
of behavior.
Researchers have gone further in their suggestions about what data analytics has to offer 
users in terms of helping users and data analysts find ways to make sense of their organizational 
experience and even to use past events as predictive of future occurrences. Systems analysts should 
be aware of these larger purposes, including retrospection, social engagement/collaboration, cue 
extraction, plausibility, and ongoing access (Namvar et al., 2016).
DATA LAKES
As you have read, data analytics are powerful tools to help decision makers make sense of 
copious amounts of formally structured data stored in data warehouses through the use of business 
intelligence. As powerful as they are, data warehouses have limitations.
The creation of data lakes reflects a response to the drawbacks users experience when 
using data warehouses. Cloud-based data lakes free developers and users from the need for 
committing to highly structured data in a (often proprietary) predetermined format. Data lakes 
allow users to simply query immense data sets that store all kinds of data. The data can be 
raw data without any formatting, normalized data suitable for a data warehouse, or something 
in between. Data lakes make use of a flat architecture and object stores with metatags and 
a one-of-a-kind, unique identifier that makes accessing and locating data accessible across 
regions easier.
Data lakes use the metaphor of a lake, as shown in Figure 13.28 (which is populated by fish, 
turtles, clams, and so on) and contain other elements such as rocks, sand, and mud. But lakes 
can also be very deep and conceal mysterious or abandoned manufactured objects. Therefore, 
we can picture data lakes as a vast collection of raw, unstructured, or semistructured data pulled 
from a multiplicity of sources, such as relational and nonrelational databases, IoT data, and 
apps of all kinds. Fortunately, a data lake can be queried for its contents or provide insights; 
its content needn’t remain concealed or mysterious. Users query data stored in a data lake to 
gain insights into system and customer behavior unavailable through structured queries of 
relational databases. The decision maker does not need to set up predetermined, structured files, 
as are mandatory for a data warehouse, in order to make use of a data lake. Data lakes support 
machine learning.
1. Data lakes are repositories for raw data and normalized and unnormalized data.
2. Data deposited in data lakes need not be structured and predefined.
3. Data lakes can be queried.
4. Data lakes are intended to make use of the vast array of data that organizations collect.
5. Data lakes can provide insights into customer behavior.
First, data lakes are repositories for raw data, normalized and unnormalized data from 
relational databases and nonrelational data from the Internet of Things, mobile devices, and apps

## PAGE 460
424    PART iV  •  THE EssEnTiALs  OF DEsign
of all kinds. Second, data deposited in data lakes are not structured and predefined before they are 
collected. Decision makers do not have to foresee what questions they may need answered from 
data in the future. Third, data lakes can be queried with simple or elaborate queries constructed 
with the help of business intelligence, data analytics, and text analytics. Furthermore, data lakes 
are intended to make use of the vast array of data that organizations collect (sometimes unobtru-
sively) but that is not readily available for decision making because of its unstructured and ubiq-
uitous nature. Finally, data lakes can provide insights into customer behavior that is unavailable 
in structured, relational databases.
From a strategic perspective, most organizations will want to develop both data warehouses 
and data lakes to leverage the data they can retrieve both internally and externally to better under-
stand their business relationships and realize their goals. Google recently announced BigLake, a 
new data lake storage engine that will make it easier for organizations to perform analysis on the 
data in their data lakes and data warehouses.
BLOCKCHAINS
A blockchain network is a data structure built on an Internet platform that permits sharing and 
creation of a digital ledger of data, which can be shared with others on a network that is either 
public or private. It is useful whenever a business or an individual wants to possess a verifiable 
electronic record to track any kind of business asset: digital currency, materials that contribute to 
a manufactured product (whether it is organic or inorganic), a tangible asset, an intangible asset, 
or a digital asset. It requires no intermediary (such as a commercial bank for digital currency) to 
conduct transactions.
Blockchains came into the public consciousness with the introduction of the crypto currency 
called bitcoins, but blockchains have many other uses. For example, a blockchain can be used 
to verify that food labeled “organic” actually arises from organic farming practices in every step 
of growing, harvesting, and packaging. Or it can be used to track the provenance of an artist’s 
work such as a painting, showing who created the painting, where and when it was painted, who 
exhibited it, who purchased the painting, and whether it is held in a private collection or in a 
museum. A big advantage of a blockchain is that it cannot be altered. IBM Blockchain provides 
a way for member banks of a global banking network to share reference data. IBM Blockchain is 
also being used to track the origin and movement of automotive parts through a complex supply 
chain including manufacturer, components, and original sources of all components that compose 
a vehicle.

CHAPTER 13  •  DEsigning D ATAb A sEs     425
Blockchain is especially useful in food supply chains for tracking perishable products. For 
instance, a blockchain would be useful to track which cows in the herd contributed milk to produc-
tion of certain numbered cartons, at what time the milk was contributed to storage vats after cows 
were milked, at what temperature the vats were maintained during storage and before milk was 
bottled. It could also track the trucks and drivers that had milk product put on their trucks and at 
what time and what locations, as well as how the milk was handled while it was on a refrigerated 
delivery truck. The blockchain ledger could reveal how long particular cases of milk product sat 
on the loading dock before being taken to coolers in the backroom of the store, and even when it 
was loaded into dairy cases for customers to purchase.
Large deviations in delivery time, temperatures in trucks, length of wait on a delivery dock, 
and temperatures in grocery store display cases can all be added to transactional data in the block-
chain. Thus, a blockchain could help pinpoint defects in perishable products (possible exposure to 
higher-than-average temperatures, or slowdown of offloading product due to a shortage of docks 
for unloading, for example) and could speed recall of products that experienced logged deviations 
in the expected supply chain.
A blockchain is an open, immutable record of transactions. Blockchains were created to 
solve problems that habitually arise from conducting transactions. They are meant to improve 
security, cut down on risk, and improve reliability and efficiency when conducting transactions 
of all sorts.
Blockchains can be thought of as distributed databases that feature controls on informa-
tion that also store information and share it. Blockchains are integrating technology platforms 
worldwide.
A proprietary, London-based firm called Blockchain makes software that allows consumers 
and businesses to make transactions using digital currencies such as bitcoin. It has made ease of 
use an important feature, explaining that an ordinary consumer who is not tech savvy can use it 
on their smartphone with no problem or special training.
Businesses use special permissioned blockchains. A permissioned blockchain offers better 
privacy, upgraded auditability, and increased efficiency for operations. A permissioned block-
chain means that transactions can be trusted, costs are lower because there is little duplication 
of effort, and the rapidity of transactions is more akin to what actually happens in business 
transactions in real time, rather than a prolonged wait for outside authorities to approve or 
update transactions before they can be executed. Many large businesses are announcing new 
blockchain-based products, including introduction of blockchain-based insurance products in 
the next few years.
Blockchain needs designers who can bridge the technical complexities of blockchain and 
make it usable for less tech-savvy customers. This includes creating interfaces that allow partici-
pants to examine information they need in the supply chain based on blockchain and being able 
to trust what they are seeing without needing to know the underlying technical complexities of 
blockchain (Schwab, 2017).
Startups such as blockgeeks.com, which is a blockchain media tech startup in Toronto, 
Canada, states: “Our mission at Blockgeeks is to teach over 10,000 developers a year in block-
chain to accelerate the ecosystem.” They are proposing to do most training online, and they are 
projecting that the demand for blockchain developers will increase rapidly over the next few years.
In the community space, there is Hyperledger (https://www.hyperledger.org), “an open 
source collaborative effort created to advance cross-industry blockchain technologies. It is a 
global collaboration, hosted by The Linux Foundation, including leaders in finance, banking, the 
IoT, supply chain, manufacturing, and technology.” Hyperledger released its updated version of 
Hyperledger Fabric, a type of distributed ledger code that will allow corporations to begin building 
blockchain-based business applications.
Four critical features of a blockchain network are (1) consensus, all parties to a transaction 
must agree that it is valid; (2) provenance, all parties to the transaction are aware of where the 
asset has come from and understand how it has changed over time; (3) immutability, once a 
transaction has been recorded to the ledger, none of the parties can tamper with it or change it  
(a separate transaction is required to remedy an error and both transactions are visible to members 
of the network); and (4) finality, there is only one place for anyone to check ownership of an asset 
or whether a transaction has been completed.

## PAGE 462
426    PART iV  •  THE EssEnTiALs  OF DEsign
Trust still poses one of the biggest organizational barriers to blockchain adoption (Clancy, 
2016). Customers and developers still must get accustomed to the idea that their supply chain 
data will not be in a central repository but will be shared across the supply chain with a variety of 
entities who have a vital interest in the information that is accessible via the blockchain. Trust in 
the capabilities of participants is also key when implementing blockchain. Participants must be 
trusted to enter transactions accurately and honestly into the blockchain ledger.
Web 3.0
While Web 2.0 is notable for interactivity, building on the original centralization of the web for 
searching, it is built on blockchain. Considering that we have just learned that blockchain engenders 
trust in secure transactions, we can say that Web 3.0 is about decentralization of data and data owner-
ship. Users will own their own data. Indeed, they will be able to monetize pieces of it, rather than just 
handing it all over to a company in exchange for a service. Many of the apps that will make Web 3.0 
a reality are not built yet, but we can sketch out how they may act when executing a transaction.
For example, if you order groceries online, the delivery service will accumulate data about 
what you purchase, how much you spend, your preferred payment, whether you pay on time or 
use buy now, pay later (BNPL), your preferred delivery date, and so on. For your part, you just 
want the groceries you ordered delivered to arrive on time and in good condition. Depending on 
their business relationships, the grocery delivery company can give or sell parts of your record to 
grocery vendors, local bakeries, delivery services, and so on.
With Web 3.0, users will own their data and eventually be able to monetize it 24/7 via the 
blockchain. However, users will not need to be involved in microtransactions such as when a large 
delivery service would like all of the preferred delivery times of every one of their customers in a 
particular zip code. If you agree to sell your data that was collected by the grocery delivery service 
but owned by you, you can sign up to do so, and those transactions will be executed automati-
cally whenever there is a purchaser of the type you have approved, typically in cryptocurrency, 
on Web 3.0, without you needing to be present or even awake for the transaction to take place. 
Whenever your data or a relevant piece of it is accessed, you will be paid.
On the other hand, you may prefer that some of the data about your food consumption to go 
to a nonprofit research hospital examining the relationship between certain foods and longevity. 
You will be able to make that specific designation of that part of your grocery store transaction, 
and you will be donating part of your data to a cause you believe in, but will not, in that case, be 
monetizing it. The point is that Web 3.0 users will be in control of who (other than the individual) 
gets their data and whether it will be monetized or not. Web. 3.0 is about “tokenization of the data” 
as well as building the data economy.
Since transactions having to do with data are automatic, you may wonder what the role of 
systems analysts will be. However, there is still plenty of room for the work of systems analysts on 
Web 3.0. Not surprisingly, many users will not be interested in or capable of setting up accounts 
and managing their data. Systems analysts can serve as consultants for organizations and users. 
In that role, you can facilitate contracts and transactions that set up and maintain users’ decentral-
ized, automated, and secure transactions on the blockchain. Analysts will also serve as translators 
for users, helping them understand the implications of Web 3.0 for their work and personal lives.
Summary
How to store data is often an important decision in the design of an information system. There are two 
approaches to storing data. The first approach is to store data in individual files, one file for each application. 
The second approach is to develop a database that can be shared by many users for a variety of applications 
as the need arises.
It is critical that everyone involved in the design of the database agrees on the value of the data and the 
cost of protecting it because securing data has costs as well as benefits. Risk assessment is an essential part 
of database planning.
An understanding of data storage requires a grasp of three realms: reality, data, and metadata. An entity 
is an object or event for which we are willing to collect and store data. Attributes are the actual characteristics 
of these entities. Data items can have values and can be organized into records that can be accessed by a key. 
Metadata describe the data and can contain restrictions about the value of a data item (such as numeric only).

## PAGE 463
CHAPTER 13  •  DEsigning D ATAb A sEs     427
Examples of conventional files include master files, table files, transaction files, work files, and report 
files. Databases typically are constructed with a relational structure. Legacy systems can have hierarchical 
or network structures, however.
Normalization is a process that transforms user views into less-complex structures called normalized 
relations. There are three steps in the normalization process. First, all repeating groups are removed. Second, 
all partial dependencies are removed. Finally, the transitive dependencies are taken out. After these three 
steps are completed, the result is the creation of numerous relations that are of third normal form (3NF).
An entity-relationship diagram may be used to determine the keys required for a record or a database 
relation. The three guidelines to follow when designing master tables or database relations are that (1) each 
separate data entity should create a master table (do not combine two distinct entities within one table), (2) 
a specific data field should exist only on one master table, and (3) each master table or database relation 
should have programs to create, read, update, and delete records.
The process of retrieving data may involve as many as eight steps: (1) choosing a relation, (2) joining two 
relations together, (3) projecting (choosing) columns, (4) selecting relevant rows, (5) deriving new attributes, 
(6) sorting or indexing rows, (7) calculating totals and performance measures, and (8) presenting the results 
to the user.
Denormalization is a process that transforms the logical data model into a physical model that is efficient 
for tasks that are most needed. Data warehouses differ from traditional databases in many ways; one is that 
they store denormalized data, which are organized around subjects. Data warehouses allow easy access via 
data-mining software, called siftware, which searches for patterns and identifies relationships not imagined 
by human decision makers.
Data mining involves using a database for more selective targeting of customers. Assuming that past 
behavior is a good predictor for future purchases, companies collect data about a person from past credit 
card purchases, driver’s license applications, warranty cards, and so on. Data mining can be powerful, but 
it may be costly, and it needs to be coordinated. In addition, it may infringe on consumer privacy or even a 
person’s civil rights.
Business intelligence is composed of features that gather and store data, as well as using knowledge 
management approaches combined with analysis. This becomes input to decision makers’ decision-making 
processes. Business analytics refers to statistical tools and models to quantitatively process structured data 
like that found in data warehouses. This in turn is input for both humans and computers. When data sets 
become too large or too complex to handle with traditional tools or within traditional databases or data 
warehouses, they are often referred to as “big data.” Data analytics use powerful algorithms to analyze vast 
amounts of structured data found in databases or to analyze huge quantities of unstructured real-time data 
that can support decision makers in making fact-based decisions.
Data lakes are repositories for raw data, normalized and unnormalized data from relational databases, 
and nonrelational data from the Internet of Things, mobile devices, and apps of all kinds. Data deposited 
in data lakes are not structured and predefined before they are collected. Data lakes can be queried to gain 
heretofore unknown insights into customers and their relationships to business goals. From a strategic per-
spective, most organizations will want to develop both data warehouses and data lakes to leverage the data 
they can retrieve both internally and externally.
A blockchain network is a data structure built on an internet platform that permits the creation of a 
digital ledger of data, which can be shared with others on a network that is either public or private. A block-
chain is an open, unchangeable record of transactions. It is useful whenever a business or individual wants to 
possess a verifiable electronic record in order to track any kind of business asset. The biggest organizational 
barrier to participating in blockchain is trust—trust that the data are safe even though they are not in a central 
repository but shared across the supply chain with interested parties, and trust that the participants in the 
supply chain will enter transactions accurately and honestly.
Web 3.0 is built on blockchain. Considering that blockchain engenders trust in secure transactions, we 
can say that Web 3.0 is about decentralization of data and data ownership. Users will own their own data. 
Indeed, they will be able to monetize pieces of it, rather than just handing it all over to a company in exchange 
for a service. Web 3.0 is just being built now, but opportunities to participate will be growing.

big data

agency has denormalized the relation. Describe the ticket ordering system by listing the data elements

Human–Computer 
Interaction and UX Design
LEARNING OBJECTIVES
Once you have mastered the material in this chapter, you will be able to:
1 Understand human–computer (HCI) interaction.
2 Appreciate the concept of usability.
3 Identify the types of user interfaces.
4 Apply user experience design when developing customer-centered experiences.
5 Discover how to design interfaces for smartphones and tablets.
6 Learn about design for intelligent personal assistants.
7 Discover designing for virtual reality and augmented reality.
8 Know the guidelines for dialog design.
9 Understand the importance of user feedback.
10 Know about the design considerations for ecommerce.
11 Formulate different types of queries that permit users to search the web.
Your awareness of human–computer interaction (HCI) and its significance to your task as a 
systems analyst is important. The next step is to master the concepts surrounding HCI and 
become proficient at assessing human information requirements and incorporating your 
findings into your designs. Ensure that systems are user centered and appropriately include 
users’ needs as well as organizational needs. Understanding HCI concepts, considering 
interfaces in light of HCI issues, and applying standard design concepts to computers in new 
ways is central to an HCI approach.
User experience (UX) design is a customer-first method for designing software. It 
is a process that observes the behavior of customers and strives to enhance customer 
satisfaction and loyalty. It accomplishes this by improving usability and ease of use. UX 
is also a design culture that emphasizes a good experience for the user over maximizing 
short-term profit.
This chapter fills in some of the details about HCI and working with users and reveals 
the potential of UX design. It also gives you some experience in applying HCI and UX 
concepts in your design of human–computer interfaces for computers, smartphones, and 
tablets; feedback; ecommerce websites; and webqueries.
14 
CHAPTER

## PAGE 469
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     433
UNDERSTANDING HUMAN–COMPUTER INTERACTION
Designing for human–computer interaction (HCI) means “ensuring system functionality and 
usability, providing effective user interaction support, and enhancing a pleasant user experience.” 
Furthermore, “the overarching goal is to achieve both organizational and individual user effec-
tiveness and efficiency. To reach these goals, managers and developers need to be knowledgeable 
about the interplay among users, tasks, task contexts, information technology (IT), and the envi-
ronments in which systems are used” (Carey et al., 2004, p. 358).
Knowledge about the interplay among users, tasks, task contexts, IT, and the environments 
in which the systems are used comprises the basis of HCI. The main tactic of HCI in systems 
analysis and design is to repeatedly elicit feedback from users about their experiences with pro-
totyped designs (which could be screens, forms, interfaces, and the like), refine the design based 
on the suggested changes, and try them with users again until the design is acceptable and until 
it is frozen by the analyst.
How Fit Affects Performance and Well-Being
Let’s begin our exploration of HCI with some useful definitions that are commonly shared among 
those working in the field.
Fit. A good fit between the HCI elements of the human, the computer, and the task that needs to 
be performed leads to performance and well-being. Just as it is important that new shoes comfort-
ably fit the shape of your foot, hold up during the activity you will be doing (such as running), 
and are made of a material (such as nylon) that is durable and cost-effective, so too is it important 
that the fit among the user, computer, and task all correspond.
Analysts want the best fit in their design. They want to make the best possible use of 
people in designing a computerized task that is intended to meet an organization objective. 
Better fit results in better performance and greater overall well-being for the human involved 
in the system.
Task. Tasks can be structured and routine, or they can be ill defined and without apparent struc-
ture. Complex tasks that require human, system, and task interaction are supported by ecommerce 
and web systems, enterprise resource planning systems, and wireless systems inside and outside 
the organization. In the foregoing chapters you have learned many methods to help you under -
stand, document, and graphically depict the tasks that people currently perform in the organization.  
listening actively is explored in a humorous way. As implied in the 
video, as a systems analyst you need to listen and show interest when 
working with users and teammates, to acknowledge key points, 
agree to listen to each other, and read reactions to your words. After 
you view the video, please reflect on the following questions:
1. What are some nonverbal ways to communicate that you are 
actively listening to a teammate or user?
2. How can you actively show interest in what a user is telling 
you during your interaction?
3. Describe some phrases that might be useful when you “agree 
to listen to each other” when talking with a user or teammate.
4. What is meant by “reading reactions to your words” in a con-
versation with a teammate? If you are receiving a confused 
reaction, what actions can you take to get the dialogue back 
on track?

This chapter focuses on human–computer inteaction, the concept of 
usablity, identifying user interfaces, user experience design, dialog 
design, and giving feedback to users. In this video, the topic of 
To view the video for this scenario,

Seven Dimensions/Boclips

## PAGE 470
434    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
You have also learned methods to help you design new tasks that will help people reach their 
objectives with the new systems you are creating.
Performance. The definition of the word performance in the HCI context is key; it refers to a 
combination of the efficiency involved in performing a task and the quality of the work produced 
by the task. For example, if analysts are creating data flow diagrams (DFDs) using high-level 
software or a computer-aided software engineering (CASE) tool with which they are proficient, 
the quality of the DFDs produced will be high. The performance is also efficient because ana-
lysts are using an automated tool with which they are familiar. They can work rapidly, with good 
results. The task fits the objective, which is to create high-quality DFDs to document a system. 
The efficiency of producing such diagrams with a CASE tool, which can then be used to store, 
retrieve, communicate, and modify the unified modeling language (UML) diagrams, is excellent 
compared to alternatives such as using a drawing tool unrelated to a data dictionary or drafting 
diagrams by hand, neither of which offers such features.
Well-Being. At this point, we introduce the concept of well-being, which is a concern for a 
human’s overall comfort, safety, and health; in sum, it is a human’s physical as well as psycho-
logical state. Does using a CASE tool for producing UML diagrams or DFDs on a computer 
serve the analyst’s well-being? Yes, because the task fits well with the analyst, the software, the 
objective, and the computer. Notice that analysts are working in an environment in which they 
are physically comfortable, are psychologically stimulated to be creative, and can be productive; 
also, each analyst’s work is valued by peers and clients, as well as being valued monetarily by 
the employing organization.
Psychological attitudes (the affective component) are important. How users feel about them-
selves and their identities, work life, and performance can all be gauged through assessing their 
attitudes. Analysts taking an HCI perspective are concerned with how attitudes color the way 
humans feel about technology and their tasks and whether their attitudes hinder or enhance their 
experience.
USABILITY
Usability is defined differently depending on which branch of science you are investigating. For 
our purposes in exploring usability through an HCI lens, we focus on usability as a way for design-
ers to evaluate the systems and interfaces they create with an eye toward addressing as many HCI 
concerns as thoroughly as possible. Usability studies (according to https://www.nngroup.com) are 
all about finding out what works in the world and what doesn’t.
Nielsen and colleagues (Nielsen & Mack, 1994; Nielsen et al., 2001) have published usability 
heuristics (or rules of thumb) based on thousands of usability tests of interfaces and, later, tests of 
ecommerce websites. They include visibility of system status, match between the system and the 
real world, user control and freedom, consistency and standards, error prevention, reconnection 
rather than recall, flexibility and efficiency of use, aesthetics, and minimalist design, help that 
users recognize, diagnosis and recovery from errors, and help and documentation.
Figure 14.1 shows a usability survey administered directly to users who have personally 
interacted with a prototype. It asks users outright about some important usability and ergonomic 
dimensions. Another approach is to write up use case scenarios for the system. These are helpful 
in examining usability concerns.
Designing for the Cognitive Styles of Individual Users
One important consideration is that data, particularly data used for decision making, need to be 
made available in different forms so users with different cognitive abilities can make sense of 
them. Some users may prefer to examine tables and make decisions, some prefer graphs, and 
others want to read narrative text.
Pivot Tables. It is possible that the same person might want different types of presentations at 
different times. For example, suppose a manager wants to compare inventory held at different 
stores in a region. A graph can present the data very effectively. A column chart can use colors to 
show when a store is near its stockout level, and it can also show the relative amount of stock by 
allowing the user to visually compare the height of the bars directly.

## PAGE 471
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     435
Suppose now that the same decision maker wants information about a particular store in a 
given month. The graphical depiction may have been set up to show the stores from highest to 
lowest inventory on a month-by-month basis. The user may prefer to return to the table that lists 
stores alphabetically, with the months listed chronologically. As you can see, the same person 
may want to see the same data in very different ways. Pivot tables allow users to arrange data in 
a table in any way they choose.
Visualization. Visualization is the representation of data as a chart, graph, or other image. Inno-
vative visual displays of data have existed for quite some time, even as early as the eighteenth 
century. Barriers to widespread use of visual displays included lack of imagination, the inability 
to draw graphs and charts in a cost-effective manner, and a lack of appreciation for such displays. 
Some charts are crammed with so much information that they become too complex for the average 
user. The consumer of information must be able to interpret the information in the diagram or it 
adds little value, so it is wise to use software to generate these charts and graphs.
Physical Considerations in HCI Design
In Chapters 11, 12, and 13 you learned the basis for sound design of screens, forms, websites, and 
databases. This included the special use of fonts, color, and layout design to communicate to users 
and to help them do the right thing with the input and output they encountered. To examine the 
underlying reasons for much of this design, it is useful to look at human sensory capabilities and 
limitations that will inform the design. In keeping with the HCI philosophy, an analyst should be 
able to compensate, overcome, or replace human senses to a varying extent.
