Vision. Systems analysts become accustomed to designing screens and reports for sighted people. 
The use of color, fonts, graphics, software, and PowerPoint presentations for displays and printed 
reports as input and output were detailed in Chapters 11 and 12. However, from an HCI perspec-
tive, analysts also think in terms of limitations on human vision. Factors such as distance from 
display to the person performing a task; the angle of the display in relation to the person viewing it; 
the size and uniformity of the characters; the brightness, contrast, balance, and glare of the screen; 
and whether a display is blinking or stable can all be designed to standards established through 
the International Organization for Standardization and other national and international groups.
Hearing. Humans have limits on the amount of stress their senses can withstand. Noisy laser 
printers, phone conversations, and shredders can lead to overload on human hearing. Office 
workers can wear noise-canceling headphones or use a personal music player, but these solutions 
may have the effect of isolating a person from the organizational setting and may even diminish 
their capability to perform tasks. Analysts need to consider noise when designing office systems.
Touch. When using an HCI perspective to evaluate the usefulness of keyboards and other input 
devices, systems analysts rate the human–computer fit as well as the dimensions examining the 
human–computer–task fit. Keyboards have been ergonomically designed to provide the correct 
feedback for a person doing data entry. Users know by the firmness of the key under their finger 
that the keystroke has been entered.
Designing for data entry using numeric keypads as the human entry device also provides a 
decision point for designers. Notice that numbers on your mobile phone are in a different order 
than numbers on a numeric keypad or a calculator. Your phone may be arranged with the numbers 
1, 2, and 3 on the top row. When you look at a calculator layout or a numeric keypad on your 
keyboard, you will see 7, 8, and 9 on the top row. Research now points to the superiority of the 
calculator layout when the user is doing a lot of data entry. However, the phone digit layout is 
supposed to be better for locating a number. Systems designers are constantly examining the fit 
between the human, the computer, and the tasks set by the organization.
Considering Human Limitations, Disabilities, and Design
All humans have limitations in their physical capabilities. Some are immediately visible, others 
are not. When designing from an HCI perspective, limitations are often discussed in terms of 
disabilities. The application of HCI to supporting and enhancing the physical capabilities 
of humans is one of the most promising application areas. Strides in biomedical engineering 
mean that there is research to support people who are blind or who have low vision, those who 
are deaf or hard of hearing, and people with limited mobility.
There are also improvements in the technical supports available to those who face difficulties 
in cognitive processing, including persons with symptoms of autism, dyslexia, and attention-
deficit disorder. Systems analysts are subject to the legal provisions of the country in which they 
are working. For instance, when designing for workplaces in the United States, you may want to 
check on the obligations of an employer on the Americans with Disabilities Act website.
One of the best ways to ensure the broadest possible accommodation is to begin designing 
from an HCI perspective. That way, your foremost concern will always be assisting a user in 
accomplishing a task, set by the organization, with the use of technology. When accommodations 
for people with disabilities are necessary, there are many sources to examine and many assistive 
devices to consider.
For people who have color vision deficiency or, more colloquially called color blindness, test 
the colors being chosen for screens or forms to be certain that they can be easily distinguished 
from each other. Particular problems occur telling the difference between red and green, for 
instance. Always design the screen or form with alternative cues, such as icons, written text, or 
audio cues that reinforce the content. For users who experience impaired hearing, make sure docu-
ments and screens include access to written versions of the audio material. Alternatively, design 
tasks in a way that headphones can be used successfully.
When designing computer tasks for those with limited mobility, think of speech input rather 
than keyboarding. In addition, new advances in biomedical engineering permit users with limited 
mobility to move the cursor on the screen by breathing into a tube or by directing the cursor to the 
desired spot on the screen by looking at that spot or even, in some highly specialized interfaces, 
by thinking about where the cursor should move.

## PAGE 473
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     437
Implementing Good HCI Practices
The ideal is to invite a usability specialist to serve on the systems development team with the other 
team members. However, many systems groups are quite small, and not many professionals are 
available who are involved in the practice of usability per se. Even if you make this recommended 
change to your project, the odds are that the position will go unstaffed or understaffed. However, 
don’t let that discourage you. You can take some simple steps that will positively influence the 
outcome of your systems project. Figure 14.2 provides a list of guidelines for taking an HCI 
approach to systems design.
FIGURE 14.2
The HCI approach to systems 
design emphasizes the fit among 
human, computer, and task.
Guidelines for the HCI Approach to Systems Design
•  Examine the task to be done and consider ﬁt among human, computer, and task.
•  Identify what obstacles exist for users in their attempts to accomplish their assigned tasks.
•  Keep in mind perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use of technology. 
•  Consider usability. Examine the usage environment by creating use case scenarios that
 depict what is going on between users and the technology. 
•  Use the information gained beforehand to ﬁgure out the physical and organizational
    environmental characteristics. Design with prototyping to accommodate diverse users and
    users with disabilities.
Start by listing about 20 different items of school-branded clothing 
for sports fans, including items such as hooded sweatshirts, T-shirts, 
baseball caps, sweatbands, running shorts, and so on. Many of 
them feature fanciful embroidered designs depicting their mascots 
in menacing or endearing poses. Hyde Park has their Golden 
Retrievers; Pierce has their much beloved bird, the Puffins; and 
St. David’s cheers with their Dragons.
Put the items into categories. Then think about what the data  
would look like. Does it make sense for Linda to look at the 
data weekly, monthly, or by semester? Will she want to look back 
five years to see whether there were any trends? Set up tables iden-
tifying the rows and columns and the content of the main cells. 
Suggest several tables so Linda can analyze them in different ways.
Now construct graphs that analyze the same data. Using some 
of the examples found in this book, suggest the type of graphs 
and show the data so that different users with different styles can 
make some decisions regarding the trend of sales over the last 
few years. Remember to compare the schools as well. Suggest 
the appropriate graphs from column, line, scatter plots, or even 
pie charts.
Also suggest three or four specific changes you would make 
to allow someone who has low vision to be able to read the graphs 
more easily. Magnification is one way to change a graph, but it may 
not be the best approach.
Consider the size of the schools because this may become the 
most important factor when determining how Linda Mascota should 
adjust her ordering for St. David’s, Hyde, and Pierce.
CONSULTING OPPORTUNITY 14.1 
School Spirit Comes in Many Sizes
L inda Mascota manages the student-alumni clothing department 
for a large bookstore in Saratoga Springs.
“Our clothing sales depend not only on whether our sports 
teams win or lose, but the overall well-being of our students and 
alums. If they are proud of their university and want to show their 
school spirit, they’ll buy up everything on our racks!” exclaims 
Linda. “But don’t underestimate the weather as a factor,” she adds. 
“If the weather turns cold in October, you’ll see a surge in people 
buying warm sweaters, hoodies, pullovers, and gloves.”
“Our store serves the major three universities in our area,” Linda 
goes on to state. “First, there is Hyde Park, what we call ‘the foot-
ball school.’ They have about 17,000 students going there. They 
have high demand for school-branded clothing, particularly in the 
fall. Then, of course, there’s Pierce University. Pierce would like 
to be considered as Ivy League, so the students like to buy crew 
and lacrosse shirts. They have about 7,500 students. Then there is 
St. David’s, with about 3,000 students. They are devoted to their 
basketball team. You’ll see sales pick up in the second semester, 
particularly during March Madness.”
Linda continues, admitting, “I thought about asking the students 
what to stock, but an email survey is out of the question. I get 
spammed a lot, so I mostly don’t bother with email. Unfortunately, 
the lead time for getting official branded sportswear into the store 
is really long, and we run the risk of stocking out. But we try to 
never run out.”
You’ve been asked to design a set of tables and graphs that 
will help analyze the sales of the bookstore’s school clothing.

## PAGE 474
438    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
Although we have been discussing the system in the abstract, it is important to recognize 
that the interface is the system for most users. However well or poorly designed, it stands as the 
representation of the system and, by reflection, your competence as a systems analyst. A well-
designed interface improves the fit among the task, the technology, and the user.
Your goal must be to design interfaces that help users and businesses get the information they 
need in and out of the system by addressing the following objectives:
1. Matching the user interface to the task.
2. Making the user interface efficient.
3. Providing appropriate feedback to users.
4. Generating usable queries.
5. Improving the productivity of computer users.
TYPES OF USER INTERFACES
In this section, several different kinds of user interfaces are described, including natural-language 
interfaces, question-and-answer interfaces, menus, form-fill interfaces, command-language inter-
faces, graphical user interfaces (GUIs), and a variety of web interfaces for use on the Internet. The 
user interface has two main components: presentation language, which is the computer-to-human 
part of the transaction, and action language, which characterizes the human-to-computer portion. 
Together, both concepts cover the form and content of the term user interface.
Natural-Language Interfaces
Natural-language interfaces are perhaps the dream and ideal of inexperienced users because 
they permit them to interact with the computer in their every day, or natural, language. No special 
skills are required of the user, who interfaces with the computer by typing or speaking a sentence 
or special commands such as “Schedule an appointment on Wednesday at 1:00 p.m. with Karla 
Salguero in marketing.” On an Apple iPhone, Siri is a voice-enabled interface that uses natural 
language. Alexa is a digital assistant used in devices such as Amazon’s Echo that can be voice-
activated. See the upcoming section on designing intelligent personal assistants for more on the 
use of natural language interfaces.
Question-and-Answer Interfaces
In a question-and-answer interface, the computer displays a question to the user on the display. 
To interact, the user enters an answer (via a keyboard stroke or a mouse click), and the computer 
then acts on that input information in a preprogrammed manner, typically by moving to the next 
question. Wizards or software setup assistants used to install software are common examples  
“I’d like to be able to make some comparisons myself. I could 
compare the turnover rate for all 12 of our warehouses. I’d also like 
to see how effectively the capacity of each of our warehouses is 
being used. Sometimes I’d like to be able to graph the comparisons 
or see a chart of them over time.”
In three paragraphs, compare three different types of interfaces that 
K. A. could use. Then recommend one interface for her use that takes 
into account her infrequent use of the PC, her enjoyment of working 
with raw data, and her desire to see data displayed in a variety of ways.
CONSULTING OPPORTUNITY 14.2 
I’d Rather Do It Myself
“I can get Raj to download any data I need from the web or our 
server to my PC,” K. A. Sharman, an upper-level manager for Yum-
time Foods (a Midwest food wholesaler), tells you. “Getting data 
is no problem. What I don’t want are a lot of reports. I’d rather get 
into the data myself.”
K. A. goes on to tell you that as an executive she doesn’t use 
her desktop PC as often as she’d like, maybe only three times a 
month, but she has some very specific ideas about what she’d like 
to do with it.

## PAGE 475
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     439
of a question-and-answer interface. The user responds to questions about the installation process, 
such as which drive to use to install the software on the system or answers questions about 
customizable features.
Menus
In using a menu, a user is limited to the options displayed. The user need not know the system but 
does need to know what task should be accomplished. For example, with a typical word processing 
menu, users can choose from the Edit, Copy, or Print options. To utilize the menu best, however, 
users must know which task they want to perform.
Menus can be nested within one another to lead a user through options in a program. Nested 
menus allow the screen to appear less cluttered, which is consistent with good design. They also 
allow users to avoid seeing menu options in which they have no interest. Nested menus can move 
users quickly through the program.
GUI menus are used to control software and have the following guidelines:
1. The main menu bar is always displayed either at the top of the application (Windows) or 
the top of the screen (Mac OS).
2. The main menu should have secondary options grouped into similar sets of features. For 
example, on a Format menu you can format a Font, Paragraph, Document, and so on. 
Each of these will be a secondary menu.
3. Menu items that are unavailable at this time should be grayed out to signify that they can-
not be used at this time.
An object menu, also called a pop-up menu or context menu, is displayed when the user clicks 
on a GUI object with the right mouse button. These menus contain items specific for the current 
activity, and most of them duplicate functions of main menu items.
Form-Fill Interfaces
Form-fill interfaces consist of onscreen forms or web-based form-fill interfaces displaying 
fields that contain data items or parameters that need to be communicated to the user. Apps such 
as Jotform, WuFoo, and Typeform make it easy to design forms, but many of the form builder 
apps allow the designer to accomplish more. Figure 14.3 provides a list of possible solutions that 
can be addressed using one of the form builder apps that go beyond simply designing a form.
as she continues the interview. “Whatever you do, though, don’t 
slow me down. One of the things I’m most proud of is that  
I can still beat the other operators. They’re good too, though,”  
Ranhita adds.
Based on this partial interview with Ranhita, what type of user 
interface will you design for her and the other operators? Assume 
that even though the new system is improved, it will still require 
massive amounts of data entry from a variety of paper medical 
insurance forms sent in by claimants.
Compare and contrast interfaces such as natural language, 
question-and-answer, menus, and web-based form-fill interfaces 
in two paragraphs. Then choose and defend one alternative. What 
qualities possessed by Ranhita and the other operators—and the 
data they will be entering—shaped your choice? Make a list of these 
qualities. Is there more than one feasible choice? Why or why not? 
Respond in a paragraph.
CONSULTING OPPORTUNITY 14.3 
Don’t Slow Me Down
“I’ve seen ’em all,” Ranhita Kulkarni tells you. “I was here 
when they got their first computer system. I guess I’ve sort of 
made a career of this,” she says cheerfully, pointing to the large 
stack of medical insurance claim forms she has been entering into 
the computer system. As a systems analyst, you are interviewing 
Ranhita, a data entry operator for AbundaCare (a large medical 
insurance company), about changes being contemplated in the 
computer system.
“I’m really fast compared with the others,” she states as she 
nods toward the six other operators in the room. “I know because 
we have little contests all the time to see who’s the fastest, with the 
fewest errors. See that chart on the wall? That shows how much 
we enter and how quickly. The gold stars show who’s the best each 
week. Performance measures are my friends.”
Ranhita says, “I don’t really mind if you change computers. 
Like I say, I’ve seen ’em all.” She resumes typing on her keyboard

## PAGE 476
440    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
Forms for display screens are set up to show what information should be input and where. 
Blank fields requiring information can be highlighted. The user moves the cursor from field to 
field by using a single stroke of an arrow or Tab key. This provides the user with good control over 
data entry. Web-based forms afford an opportunity to include hyperlinks to examples of correctly 
filled-out forms or to further help and provide examples.
Form input for displays can be simplified by supplying default values for fields and then 
allowing users to modify default information, if necessary. For example, a database management 
system designed to show a form for inputting checks may supply the next sequential check number 
as a default when a new check form is exhibited. If checks are missing, the user changes the check 
number to reflect the actual check being input.
Input for display screen fields can be alphanumerically restricted so that, for example, users 
can enter only numbers in a field requesting a Social Security number, or they can input only 
letters where a person’s name is required. If numbers are input where only letters are allowed, the 
computer may alert the user via audio output that the field was filled out incorrectly.
Web forms can return incomplete forms to the user with an explanation of what data must 
be entered to complete the transaction. Often, fields with missing data are marked in red, and the 
user is required to complete the entry before moving on to the next web page.
Web-based documents can be sent directly to billing if a transaction is involved, or they 
can go directly to a real-time database if a survey is being submitted. Web-based forms push the 
responsibility for accuracy to the user and make the form available for completion and submission 
on a 24-hour, seven-day-a-week, worldwide basis.
Choosing and Evaluating Interfaces
When choosing and evaluating interfaces, keep these standards in mind:
1. The necessary training period for users should be acceptably short.
2. Early in their training, users should be able to enter commands without thinking about 
them or without referring to a help menu or manual. Keeping interfaces consistent through-
out applications can help in this regard.
3. The interface should be seamless so errors are few and those that do occur are not occur-
ring because of poor design.
4. The time users and the system need to bounce back from errors should be short.
5. Infrequent users should be able to relearn the system quickly.
Many different interfaces are available, and it is important to realize that an effective interface 
goes a long way toward addressing key HCI concerns. Users should want to use the system, and 
they should find it attractive, effective, and pleasing to use.
UX DESIGN
UX design (user experience design)  is a customer-first method for designing software that 
observes the behavior of customers and strives to enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty. It 
accomplishes this by improving usability and ease of use, but it also understands the customer 
must enjoy interaction with the product.
FIGURE 14.3
Many form design apps allow a 
systems designer to achieve useful 
solutions beyond the form itself.
Advantages of Using Commercially Available Form Design Apps Include:
• Forms can be developed easily using drag-and-drop.
• Forms can include logic jumps that automatically skip over irrelevant questions.
• Forms can capture photos and drawings and can accept payments.
• Output from forms can be used to create dashboards.
• Forms can be routed to speciﬁc ﬁeld staff or customers and be tracked.
• Forms can provide real-time notiﬁcations when someone completes a form.
• Forms can be integrated with services like PayPal, mailchimp, and Zoom.
• Form design apps can use responses from earlier answers in later questions.
• Forms can accept signatures.

## PAGE 477
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     441
There is a difference between user interface (UI or GUI) design and user experience design. 
UI involves designing for smooth interaction with emphasis on layout, menus, and buttons for 
input, and graphs, charts, and tables for output. UX designs are for users’ requests and needs, 
human-centered flow, and, most important, usability.
There are many contrasts and comparisons between a traditional approach and a UX design 
approach. The top layer of Figure 14.4 shows how traditional designers pull strategic objectives 
from top management to guide information systems development. The designer works with the 
strategic objectives as outlined, formulates a design for the information systems, and then pushes 
that design to the user who is often in middle or operational levels of the organization. The 
bottom layer of the figure shows the UX design approach. The designer interacts with users to 
gather their wants and needs, designs an information system after interacting with users and the 
proposed technology, then shares the resulting user objectives with management in the newly 
designed system.
Quality pioneer Dr. Joseph Juran said you could judge quality by whether a product can be 
used the way it was intended. This turns out to be at the heart of UX design. Usability means that 
a system is easy to understand, easy to use, and easy to appreciate. A few simple features, such 
as the ability for the user to save a password, might make the difference.
The UX designer considers these issues:
1. Have the user’s needs been met without any complications?
2. Does the workflow proceed without requiring any extra or duplicate steps?
3. Is the user able to complete the task without being forced to recall something from an ear-
lier step?
4. If the user makes a mistake, can it be corrected easily?
5. Is the interaction natural to the user?
6. Can the user be more efficient by using shortcuts?
7. Does the user feel in control of the system?
FIGURE 14.4
In traditional design, strategic 
objectives motivate the design 
effort, whereas in user experience 
design, the user motivates 
the design effort.
Designer
pushes design
to the user .
Designer pulls
strategic
objectives from
management.
Designer gathers
user wants and
needs.
Designer shares
user objectives
with management.
User Experience (UX) Design
Traditional Design

## PAGE 478
442    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
To design the user experience, a designer first has to know about people. Although the 
designer doesn’t need a degree in psychology, the designer does need to have some knowledge 
about how people become motivated, what makes a person demonstrate loyalty, what makes a 
person happy, and how people make decisions.
In order to appreciate people more, the designer needs to gather information about people. 
This is covered in more detail in Chapters 4 and 5, and it requires the analyst designer to master 
interactive and unobtrusive methods, including both quantitative and qualitative approaches.
When trying out new features or redesigning the site, prototypes prove to be extremely help-
ful in getting reactions from users. This subject is covered extensively in Chapter 6. New designs 
can start with a prototype. Even user reactions to a nonoperational prototype can elicit lots of 
information from the user.
Having a group of users walk through the current system is another way to gather useful infor-
mation. If you ask the users to walk through a website and observe how easy or difficult it is for 
them to navigate the site, that is crucial information to their experience. If you ask users to use an 
app to perform a series of actions, it will show where the user stumbles, forgets, or gets frustrated.
When practicing UX design for websites, keep in mind the following 10 key adages listed in 
Figure 14.5. You will notice that each of the dos and don’ts encourage the designer to be observant 
of the user throughout the designed workflow and to be honest with the user in clearly delineating 
advertisements from actual content.
Five Designer Actions That Promote Good UX Design
Do Create Simple and Stress Free Sign-Ins. Do not require something out of the ordinary. 
Although having a user sign in using Facebook may yield additional data about the person, the 
individual may not want to give you that information. In the past, some games in the Apple App 
Store only notified users that a Facebook sign-in was required after they purchased the game. 
This was extremely rare, but the experience rattled many users who demanded their money back.
Do Make the Default Choices User Friendly. If you ask users whether they agree to send infor -
mation back to the company, make the default “No.” Observe all regulations in this regard as 
these can vary by country or region (for example, European Union rules versus guidelines from 
the United States). If you want users to have a good experience, don’t trick them by making the 
default “Yes.” Also, if you use colors, make sure green means “agree” or “yes” and red means 
“disagree” or “no.” Also, countries or regions differ on whether companies must show users an 
“opt-in” or an “opt-out” option.
Do Design to Specs that Describe the Majority of Your Users. Don’t assume your users have 
the latest hardware and software updates. Companies have gone out of business improving their 
product. They added features to the product but assumed that users had the latest hardware. Users 
whose computers slowed to a crawl were disenchanted and removed the product from their systems 
forever. One bad experience can change the destiny of the company developing the app or system.
Do Provide a Way Out. If you decide on a subscription model, make sure users can easily get out 
of the agreement. Often a service will begin with a free 30-day trial but collect credit card informa-
tion as soon as the trial starts. Before users notice, they are into the second month. If you want to 
have a good user experience, warn them that their free trial is ending. If they continue, make sure 
FIGURE 14.5
A list of dos and don’ts for 
practicing good UX design.
Do’s and Don’ts of UX Design
Do’s Don’ts
•  Do create simple and stress free sign-ins
•  Do make the default choices user friendly
•  Do design to specs that describe the majority
    of users
•  Do provide a way out
•  Do keep your eye on users
•  Don’t masquerade ads as content
•  Don’t block the user from seeing the
    content
•  Don’t ask the user for too many
    permissions
•  Don’t spring surprises at checkout
•  Don’t stop testing

## PAGE 479
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     443
they can stop their subscription easily. Consider giving them a link for ending the subscription 
when they first subscribe.
Do Keep Your Eye on Users. Users also change. They change their workflows, their preferences, 
and their hardware. Average users now look at their smartphone 150 times a day, but in a few 
years wearable technology may change these patterns. UX design is user centered, so above all 
keep monitoring your users and deliver a positive user experience.
Let’s turn now to five actions that UX designers should avoid so they do not frustrate or thwart 
users as they learn and continue to work with software applications. Keeping users at the center 
of UX design and properly empathizing with users who must learn new software will help you 
avoid these design don’ts.
Five Designer Actions to Avoid in UX Design
Don’t Masquerade Ads as Content. Interspersed between paragraphs on a website, there are often 
links that appear to lead to descriptions of products, other articles, or an offer to obtain more detail 
about a subject. Many of these are links to ads. The user feels deceived.
Don’t Block the User from Seeing the Content. A number of news sources block users after they 
have viewed a certain number of news stories per month, but that doesn’t lead to a satisfying 
experience. Even worse, some websites force users to disable an ad blocker before proceeding. We 
all can understand that websites need to sell ads to be profitable, but websites will have a difficult 
time attracting new users if they present the user with a bad experience.
Don’t Ask the User for Too Many Permissions. Asking users if they want to receive notifications 
as soon as they enter the site is unfriendly. Also, asking users to have access to the camera or their 
address book (or contacts) is poor design as well. If there is a legitimate reason for having access 
to the camera or address book, explain it at the outset.
Don’t Spring Surprises at Checkout. It is easy to find bargains on the Internet. Some companies 
will add unexpected costs or fees at checkout. These bad surprises can be extra shipping costs. If 
the item purchased is a service, or tickets for a concert to be held at the box office, “transaction 
fees” might be applied. Transactions will not be completed at this point if the user learns about 
unanticipated costs that are unreasonable or unforeseen. One online food delivery service in our 
area changed the default charge to include giving a driver an 18 percent tip. Because drivers do not 
do the same things servers in restaurants do, the delivery company upset many of its customers.
Don’t Stop Testing. Operating systems change. Make sure you keep up with the changes. If you 
allow information to be exchanged with other applications, make sure they still work.
We all realize that the company providing the website or application needs to make a profit 
or needs to deliver a message. Even so, when you adopt the principles of UX design, make sure 
users have a good experience. Avoid making users feel uncomfortable or awkward; instead, make 
them feel satisfied that they spent their time wisely in using the website or app.
UX Design Guidelines: An Ecommerce Example
Here are some good practices derived from UX design principles that can help in designing a 
successful ecommerce transaction:
1. Explain the shipping costs early and make them easy to discover. Allow users to see 
the types of shipping options (UPS, FedEx, USPS, and so on) and their typical costs 
before they check out. Include a dedicated tab so users can see how long shipping takes 
and the available choices.
2. Update the prices in a Save for Later area dynamically and provide a note to users.  
A change in price (either up or down) can remind users to act. Updates keep users engaged, 
reminds them they have merchandise saved in a cart, and spurs them to action.
3. Allow customers to remove items from their shopping cart or bag and to easily change 
the quantities of items in the cart. Several small buttons or drop-down lists can facilitate 
these actions. The addition of a button that says Edit allows ecommerce customers to change

## PAGE 480
444    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
their order (choosing a different size or color, for example), a button that says Remove 
allows them to take it out of their shopping cart, and a miniature drop-down list next to the 
item in the cart labeled Quantity can let them change the quantity ordered incrementally to 
add or subtract items without going back into the original page that displayed the item.
4. Clearly indicate credit card fees and transaction fees. It should be clear to the customer 
how much the transaction would cost (certain credit cards or payment options may be more 
expensive than others). Also, if there are handling fees for any services, such as holding 
concert tickets at the box office, that should be noted as well.
5. Allow customers to continue shopping or proceed to checkout after an item has been 
placed in the shopping cart or bag. Once customers have added an item to their cart or  
bag, two options should be offered using two separate buttons: one that says Continue 
Shopping, another that says Proceed to Checkout. If these two choices are too stark, the 
developer can add buttons for Save for Later or Add to Wish List.
6. As customers move to the next page during Checkout, and before they enter payment infor-
mation, remind them of the transaction details. For example, for an online purchase of live 
event tickets, this reminder can include date and time of the concert, the artist performing, 
the quantity of tickets, their location, and cost. For purchase of clothing or other items, this 
screen should provide a summary of a shopping bag or cart (a list of items and total costs). 
Some shopping carts show a thumbnail of the item being ordered as well. Consider adding 
a 24/7 chat option to answer any questions prior to checkout so that the purchase will be 
completed. If a human is unavailable around the clock, you can develop a chatbot using 
one of several toolkits available, or even scale up to a hybrid that combines people and a 
bot, combining a high level of artificial intelligence (Heckstall, 2017).
Benefits of UX Design
If you base your design of ecommerce transactions on UX design practices, there are many 
benefits to gain:
1. Tasks can be completed more quickly by the customer. The organization of the 
ecommerce site will appear logical to the user, it will flow well, and the customer will 
be able to follow the flow of the process from browsing items, to selecting them, placing 
items in a cart or bag, returning to the website to make additional selections, exploring 
shipping and payment costs, to checking out with printable receipts, clearly posted return 
policies, and notifications of expected delivery dates.
2. More tasks can be completed in a given amount of customer time. At a website 
designed from a UX design perspective, the tasks to be accomplished online can be 
arranged efficiently, so customers needing an expedited checkout are supported (suppose a 
sale ends at midnight and they are still shopping at 11:55 p.m.) as well as those who want 
to take a more leisurely approach by comparing items and prices they have added to their 
shopping bag or cart over the course of a few days.
3. Task results can be more accurate. Customers will be prompted for information in the 
expected order, with some nonsensitive account information capable of automatically 
repopulating during checkout, so there is little room for transcription errors to creep into 
the transaction.
4. Less support is needed to address user complaints arising out of a poor user 
experience. Using UX design principles for ecommerce transactions means that the user 
is at the center of the online shopping experience. UX designers develop many different 
scenarios and paths through the website, all of which can end in a successful purchase. 
Having examined what can go wrong with the user experience and having sought remedies 
beforehand, poor user experiences become rare. The organization is free to devote more 
time to maintaining customer relationships and less time unraveling problems arising out  
of dissatisfaction with the user experience.
5. Users will complete the transaction rather than abandoning their cart and leaving 
the site. Designers have an opportunity to develop experiences that support the completion 
of the transaction in a satisfactory way, supporting numerous paths through the website 
and making checkout an option on every page. One encouragement to purchase added

## PAGE 481
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     445
to a shopping bag can express a reminder similar in tone to the Nordstrom shopping bag 
message that states: Popular items sell out fast. Adding an item here doesn’t hold it, so get 
what you love before it’s gone.
6. Customers will remain loyal and not seek other websites or software. If customers have 
a good user experience, they are likely to return for multiple visits. The interface is familiar 
and logical, checking out is efficient and pleasant, the customer is recognized, and the cus-
tomer has an account that can auto-fill many pieces of information to speed up checkout on 
the personalized pages. Many customers avoid setting up accounts on new sites and would 
far prefer to return to known sites that offer great user experiences.
7. The user will be more satisfied with the overall experience. UX design means that the 
customer is at the center of the website or software development. Looking at the ecom-
merce transaction through a customer’s eyes helps developers create an experience that is 
satisfactory from the start of the sign-on all the way to purchase and even through to mer-
chandise arrival and possible return.
DESIGNING INTERFACES FOR SMARTPHONES AND TABLETS
Smartphones and tablets open a new creative approach for designers. Touch-sensitive screens 
allow a user to use a finger to activate the display. The operating systems for these small devices 
use multitouch gestures (capacitive sensing featuring screens controlled with a human finger 
or a conductive stylus) for moving from one screen to another or from one state to another on 
the same screen. An example of the latter is enlarging a photo by flicking your fingers outward.
Gestures
Some gestures are basic. All humans start poking at objects before they know what a computer is; 
nudging and poking are inherent. It is also intuitive to swipe your finger from right to left when 
you need to turn a page when reading English, for example.
Using a pinching gesture to zoom in and out to shrink or enlarge a photo or to zoom in and 
out on a map is not intuitive. Once people learn it, however, they remember it. Somehow it makes 
sense to people.
Designers can take advantage of the fact that people easily adapt to new interfaces if they 
make sense. When you choose existing gestures or design new gestures for an app you are devel-
oping, make sure it is based on how most people prefer to use the gesture. Chances are that they 
won’t think exactly like you. You have thought about the gesture; they have not. They may not 
discover a designed gesture on their own.
On a desktop, arrow keys are used to go from one screen to the next and using a scroll bar is 
the common way to get to the bottom of a news story on the web. On smartphones and tablets, a 
swipe may be more logical when going from left to right and a tug may be the preferred way to 
look down further at an article or a list.
When a designer creates new nonstandard gestures, problems can occur. Suppose that a user 
has other apps that use a swipe from left to right as a copy command, and you develop a calcula-
tor app where a swipe from left to right clears the display. A user who owns an app that regularly 
requires the copy gesture will likely be upset by your adoption of the gesture for something dif-
ferent (and potentially destructive of data). You just lost a customer.
Then there is the temptation to take advantage of the latest techniques without really think-
ing through what the user would like to actually do. One example of this is the use of a motion 
detector that detects motion in three dimensions, called an accelerometer, to change something 
in the app by shaking the smartphone. Shaking is incorporated into apps for games, seismometers, 
pedometers, and apps that give biofeedback. In those contexts, the designer has made a choice 
that makes sense to the user.
Remember, however, that shaking is not an obvious or innate gesture. Although many users 
adapted to shaking their iPhone, fewer users wanted to shake their iPad. The size of the platform 
and the context of the app mattered. Also, shaking the iPhone may look odd at the office or on 
public transportation. If a designer still feels that shaking is highly useful for some apps, the 
designer should add an option for users uncomfortable with it. Pressing part of a screen or a button 
is subtle. Shaking is not.

## PAGE 482
446    PART IV  •  THE EssEnTIALs  oF DEsIgn
Designers need to think about the metaphor before programming a gesture simply because 
it exists. Urbanspoon (an app that located and recommended restaurants) introduced an app that 
allowed the user to randomly select a restaurant, using the metaphor of spinning a slot machine; 
however, some users might not think the gambling metaphor adequately reflects their quest for 
fine dining.
Remember, too, that every gesture needs to provide the user with feedback. If the user tugs 
and nothing more is in the list, it needs to appear obvious to the user that they have reached the end 
of the list. If the user turns a page, the designer should show motion so that the user understands 
that the page is turning. Feedback is discussed in the section “Feedback for Users.”
In Chapter 11, we discussed designing for both portrait and landscape modes. Make sure that 
the gestures are the same for both orientations.
Alerts, Notices, and Queries
Alerts, notices, and queries are forms of output on smartphones and tablets. An alert is useful if 
a severe thunderstorm is approaching, but if the user’s phone is on during a theater performance, 
the user may be asked to leave. Alerts can include a sound but remember that they do not need to 
do so to still be helpful.
Alerts are for critical information that the user needs to know in a timely manner. It is not a 
good idea to use an alert to tell the user that signal strength is low, for instance. That’s what the 
signal bars are for.
Notices convey information to a user. Some of that information, such as the notification that 
an update to the app is ready, may not be urgently important. In this context it breaks the user’s 
concentration, so a notification such as an update might be best kept as part of the app launch 
process but not used as an alert.
Some developers like to include queries  such as “Would you like to rate this app today?” 
This makes the designer seem less professional and, by including it, you could annoy the very 
people you are trying to please.
Always give the user a chance to opt out. Many users just do not want to be notified with 
information or alerts. Users can change their notification settings in the “Settings” area at any 
time, but it’s nice if they have a chance to opt out during the first launch of the app.
Badges
Another feature of smartphones and tablets is the ability to put “badges” on home screen icons. 
On the iPhone and iPad, these are little red circles that appear to be affixed to the icon for the app. 
A badge for the App Store signifies how many updates are waiting for the user to download and 
install. Some weather apps use a badge to display the current temperature.
Badges are good because they are an unobtrusive way to send a message to the user. They are quiet 
and passive, not loud and active like alerts and notifications. One problem is that they are ignored; 
another is that they may be outdated. (For example, the temperature displayed as a badge on your 
weather app may indicate the last time you opened the app sometime last week, not the temperature 
for today.) So unless you have something meaningful to convey, try to avoid using a badge.
DESIGN FOR INTELLIGENT PERSONAL ASSISTANTS
Intelligent personal assistants, also known as virtual assistants, are software agents that can 
accept commands written or spoken from users and perform tasks based on that input. They are 
not to be confused with personal digital assistants, which are hardware devices that were popular 
before introduction of the more capable smartphones.
Familiar intelligent personal assistants are Siri (from Apple), Google Assistant, Amazon 
Alexa, and Microsoft Cortana. In 2012 Apple introduced Siri, billed as your personal assistant 
that “helps you get things done, just by asking.” Users can speak to Siri in natural language, just 
as they would talk to a friend. Unlike older voice recognition systems, Siri does not need to be 
taught to respond to your voice commands.
Intelligent personal assistants have been available on smartphones for some time, but usage 
increased with the introduction of smart speakers that are connected to smartphones or computers 
via Bluetooth or Wi-Fi. Intelligent personal assistants can be found on desktops, smartwatches,

## PAGE 483
CHAPTER 14  •  HUmAn–ComPUTER  InTERACTIon AnD  UX DEsIgn     447
Facebook messenger, in cars and appliances, and even in apps (such as “Dom,” the personal assis-
tant within the Domino’s Pizza app). Intelligent personal assistants can recognize natural-language 
voice commands and answer questions. Some of the tasks they can perform are listed in Figure 14.6.
Developers can design for intelligent personal assistants using platforms such as Apple’s 
SiriKit, Actions on Google, or Amazon Lex. It is expected that intelligent personal assistants will 
continue to learn by using machine learning and other AI techniques.
Developing simple actions using application program interfaces is not that difficult. Complex 
actions will take some time to develop and must be continually maintained. It is therefore a 
strategic decision whether a company should design for use by intelligent personal assistants. 
Questions that need to be asked are:
1. Will the adoption of an intelligent personal assistant be consistent with our strategic 
objectives?
2. Will use of an intelligent personal assistant give our customers a better experience?
3. Will use of an intelligent personal assistant expand our customer base?
4. Which platform is most appropriate for our users?
5. Which voice should speak for our brand?
6. Can we develop our voice interface in-house or do we need to outsource it to those more 
skilled in designing voice interfaces?
Before the development team jumps in and starts developing an interface with an intelligent 
personal assistant, these questions need to be addressed. The six questions posed in the list above 
are all business-oriented questions. “Which platform is most appropriate for our users?” will have 
technical considerations, but a company first has to decide whether company values are consistent 
with hosting the intelligent personal assistant. For example, one intelligent personal assistant was 
recently scrutinized for privacy concerns. If customers do not trust the intelligent personal assis-
tant, would they trust your company and its use of that intelligent personal assistant?
The question “Which voice speaks for your brand?” can be rephrased “What should your 
company representative sound like?” Which gender should the voice have? Should its voice be 
recognized as originating from a certain country or region? Should it use youthful pronunciation 
and slang terminology? Should it explain everything or assume the user is knowledgeable and 
experienced? Much needs to be decided, and this requires input from executives, technical staff, 
customers, and potential customers before proceeding with a company relationship with an intel-
ligent personal assistant.
DESIGNING FOR VIRTUAL REALITY AND  
AUGMENTED REALITY
Virtual reality (VR) is an artificial, totally computer-generated world. The user interacts with 
this artificial world and in doing so is completely immersed in the experience through vision 
and hearing.
Augmented reality (AR) combines artificial elements with the real world. A developer 
creates images that appear to be realistic 3-D objects that can blend into the real world. The 
images exist in layers and allow the user to interact with the artificial objects.
FIGURE 14.6
Developers need to be aware of the 
many different tasks an intelligent

Make phone calls

Stream podcasts
