tion in the event of a disaster that can range in scope from 
a self-service to fully managed offering. (3)
Distributed Conversion It refers to a situation in 
which many installations of the same system are contem-
plated, as is the case in banking or in franchises such as 
restaurants or clothing stores. One entire conversion is 
done (with any of the four approaches considered previ-
ously) at one site. When that conversion is successfully 
completed, other conversions are done for other sites. (16)
Document Type Definition (DTD) A DTD is 
used to determine whether the XML document content is 
valid—that is, whether it conforms to the order and type 
of data that must be present in the document. The DTD is 
easy to create and well-supported by standard software. 
Once the DTD has been completed, it may be used to vali-
date the XML document using standard XML tools. The 
DTD is easier to create if a data dictionary has been com-
pleted because the analyst has worked with users and made 
decisions on the structure of the data. (8)
Domain Integrity These rules are used to validate 
the data, such as table, limit, range, and other validation 
checks. (13)
Dual-Factor Authentication A security process 
where users give two unique authentication factors such as 
a password and a one-time code to verify their authoriza-
tion to access a system or software (16)
Drop-Down List A GUI design element that permits 
users to click on a box that appears to drop down on the 
screen and list a number of alternatives, which can be sub-
sequently chosen. (12)
Dynamic Web Pages Web pages that change 
themselves as the result of user action. They often use 
 JavaScript to modify some part of the web page or a style. 
Changing an image when the mouse moves over it or 
rotating random images at a given time interval are com-
mon examples of dynamic web pages. The web page may

## PAGE 582
546    GLOSSARY
detect the width of the browser window and modify the 
page accordingly. Menus that expand when the user clicks 
a small plus sign to the left of the menu or when the mouse 
moves over a menu are other examples of dynamic web 
pages. (12)
Earned Value Earned value (EV) is an estimate of the 
value of the work performed thus far. EV refers only to the 
work that has been completed to date. (3)
Earned Value Management A technique to help 
determine progress (or setbacks) of a project and involves 
project cost, the project schedule, and the performance of 
the project team. (3)
Ecommerce Project Management Differs from 
other systems project management in that data are scat-
tered across the organization; more staff from across the 
organization is needed. Ecommerce systems must be inte-
grated into existing organizational systems. Security of 
ecommerce systems is of the utmost importance. (3)
Economic Feasibility Economic feasibility is the 
second part of resource determination. The basic resources 
to consider are your time and that of the systems analysis 
team, the cost of doing a full systems study (including the 
time of employees you will be working with), the cost of 
the business employee time, the estimated cost of hard-
ware, and the estimated cost of software, software develop-
ment, or software customization. (3)
Electronic Output System output that includes web-
pages, email, RSS (Real Simple Syndication) feeds, and 
blogs. (11)
Email Electronic mail (email or e-mail) is a method of 
exchanging messages between people using electronic 
devices. (11)
Email Filtering Products Products that provide 
policy-based email and email attachment scanning and 
filtering to protect companies against both incoming and 
outgoing email. Incoming scanning protects against spam 
(unsolicited email such as advertising) attacks, and outgo-
ing scanning protects against the loss of proprietary infor-
mation. (16)
Encryption Software A type of security program that 
enables encryption and decryption of a data stream at rest 
or in transit. (16)
End User License Agreement (EULA) A EULA 
gives others the right to use a developer’s app and limits 
what a user can do with the app. App developers should 
create their own EULAs. (11)
Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) Systems  
ERP systems standardize, streamline, and integrate  
processes across a wide range of business functions. (1)
Enterprise Systems or Enterprise Resource 
Planning (ERP) Information systems that are 
integrated organization-wide (enterprise-wide) that help 
companies in the coordination of critical organizational 
processes. (2)
Entity A person, group, department, or system that either 
receives or originates information or data. One of the 
 primary symbols on a data flow diagram. (13) See also 
data flow diagram, external entity.
Entity Classes Entity classes represent real-world 
items, such as people, things, and so on. Entity classes are 
the entities represented on an entity-relationship (E-R) 
 diagram. (10)
Entity Integrity The entity integrity constraints are 
rules that govern the composition of primary keys. (13)
Entity Subtype A special one-to-one relationship 
used to represent additional attributes (fields) of another 
entity that may not be present on every record of the first 
entity. (13)
Entity-Relationship (E-R) Model A graphical rep-
resentation of an E-R model. (2)
Environment Anything external to an organization. 
Multiple environments exist, such as the physical, eco-
nomic, legal, and social environments. (2)
Epic An epic is a compendium of user stories from cus-
tomers and end users. Epics can be used to organize all 
of the user stories and break down the tasks into release 
 versions. (6)
Estimate to Complete (ETC) ETC considers 
how the team has done in the past and estimates how 
much additional money will be required to complete 
the project at its current rate of performance. It is calcu-
lated by subtracting the earned value from the budget at 
completion. (3)
EU General Data Protection Regulation 
(GPDR) A European Union regulation on data protec-
tion and privacy in the EU and the European Economic 
Area that governs cookie permissions. (14)
Event An event is an input to the system that happens at a 
specific time and place and causes the system to do some-
thing. (10)
Event-Response Chart When there are complicated 
interactions on a web form (or any other GUI form), an 
event-response chart may be used to list the variety of 
events that can occur. Event-response charts may be used at 
a high level to model business events and responses. (12)
Event Response Table Events are usually summarized 
in an event response table. (7)
Estimate at Completion (EAC) EAC represents the 
revised project budget. It is the amount the entire project or 
task will cost when it is finished. It is calculated by adding 
the actual cost to the estimated amount of money it will 
take to complete the project. (3)
Expediting Speeding up a process is called expediting, 
and sometimes systems analysts or project managers may

## PAGE 583
GLOSSARY     547
benefit from completing a project much faster, such as 
receiving a bonus if you finish sooner. (3)
Exploded An exploded (or decomposed) data flow 
 diagram is one that is broken into smaller parts for 
 analysis. (7)
Exploration Phase The beginning phase of agile devel-
opment, where an analyst asserts his or her conviction that 
the agile approach is the correct one and then assembles a 
development team and assesses their skills. This phase can 
last for a few weeks or up to a few months. (1)
Extensible Markup Language (XML) Extensible 
markup language (XML) can be used to exchange data 
between businesses or between systems within a business. 
It is similar to HTML, the markup language used to create 
web pages, but XML is more powerful. (8)
Extensible Stylesheet Language Transformations  
(XSLT) One of the advantages of using hypertext 
markup language is that an XML document can be trans-
formed into different output media types. This is done 
using CSSs or extensible stylesheet language transforma-
tions (XSLT). These methods reinforce the idea that data 
can be defined once and used many times in different 
 formats. (11)
External Entity A source or destination of data consid-
ered to be external to the system being described in a data 
flow diagram. Also called an entity. (7) See also data flow 
diagram.
Extreme Programming (XP) A systems develop-
ment approach that accepts what we know as good systems 
development practices and takes them to the extreme. The 
genesis of agile approaches. (6)
Fat Footers A website footer that can act as an addi-
tional navigation option for a webpage, displaying impor-
tant links such as a sitemap of the website and corporate 
contact information. (12)
Feedback Feedback is one form of system control. As 
systems, all organizations use planning and control to man-
age their resources effectively. System outputs are used as 
feedback that compares performance with goals. This com-
parison, in turn, helps managers formulate more specific 
goals as inputs. (2)
Firewall Computer security software used to erect a 
barrier between an organization’s LAN and the internet. 
Although it prevents hackers from getting into an internal 
network, it also stops organizational members from getting 
direct access to the internet. (16)
First Normal Form (1NF) The first step in normaliz-
ing a relation in data used in a database so that it contains 
no repeating groups. (13) See also second normal form, 
third normal form.
First-of-a-Series Prototype A third conception 
of prototyping involves creating a first full-scale model 
of a system, often called a first-of-a-series prototype or 
pilot. (6)
Fit The way that HCI elements of a human, a computer, 
and a task that needs to be performed work together to 
improve performance and well-being. (14)
Flat Web Design The use of two-dimensional images 
to portray objects on a website without shadowing. (11) 
See also skeuomorphic design for a contrast in design 
approaches.
Folklore A system documentation technique based on 
traditional methods used in gathering information about 
people and legends. (16)
Forecasting System analysts’ predictions about certain 
key variables before the systems proposal is submitted to 
the client. Forecasting is the art and science of predicting 
key variables, often assisted with mathematical forecasting 
models. (3)
Fork Parallel activities and may have one event going into 
the synchronization bar and several events going out of it, 
called a fork. (10)
Form-Fill Interface Part of GUI design elements that 
automatically prompt the user to fill in a standard form. 
Useful for ecommerce applications. (14)
Form Flow It can minimize the time and effort employ-
ees and other users expend in form completion. In 
 English-speaking cultures, forms should flow from left to 
right and top to bottom. (12)
Form Values They are used in calculations performed 
using JavaScript on the browser to multiply, add, and make 
decisions. (12)
Forty-Hour Workweek Forty-hour workweek means 
that agile development teams purposely endorse a cultural 
core practice in which the team works intensely together 
during a typical 40-hour workweek. (6)
Function Codes The functions that an analyst or a pro-
grammer desires a computer to perform with data. (15)
Function Point Analysis A way to estimate project 
size, considering five main components of computer sys-
tems: external inputs, external outputs, external log que-
ries, internal logical files, and external interface files. (3)
Functionally Primitive Processes may or may not be 
exploded, depending on their level of complexity. When a 
process is not exploded, it is said to be functionally primi-
tive. (7)
Funnel Structure Funnel structure method provides an 
easy, nonthreatening way to begin an interview. A funnel-
shaped question sequence is also useful when the inter-
viewee feels emotional about the topic and needs freedom 
to express those emotions. (4)
Gantt Chart A graphical representation of a project that 
shows each task activity as a horizontal bar, the length of 
which is proportional to its time for completion. (3)

## PAGE 584
548    GLOSSARY
General Public License (GPL) GPL is a series of 
widely used free software licenses that guarantee end users 
the four freedoms to run, study, share, and modify the 
 software. (11)
Gen/Spec Diagram An offshoot of a class diagram is 
a gen/spec diagram (which stands for generalization/spe-
cialization). (10)
Gestures A variety of movements for a finger or a capac-
itive device such as a stylus across a touch-sensitive screen 
on a smartphone or tablet that enables the user to change 
screens, enlarge or shrink content with a pinch, zoom in or 
out of a page, or rotate or scroll content. Used with apps 
for smartphones and tablets. (11)
Gradual or Phased Conversion It attempts to 
combine the best features of the two previously mentioned 
plans without incurring all the risks. In this plan, the vol-
ume of transactions handled by the new system is gradu-
ally increased as the system is phased in. (16)
Green IT With the advent of green IT (also called green 
computing or ICT sustainability), many organizations are 
trying to encourage customers to think about environmen-
tal savings, ease of use, and 24-hour access to accounts so 
that they switch from paper statements to online bills. (11)
Halo Effect The halo effect is a problem that arises 
when the impression formed in one question carries into 
the next question. (4)
Hamburger Icon/Hamburger Menu The ham-
burger icon or hamburger menu is depicted with three 
stacked bars that minimalistically resemble a hamburger 
patty placed inside a bun. The three bars are stacked 
together in a parallel position within a square as part of 
a GUI. Place this icon at the top of a graphical display to 
alert the user that they can click on the hamburger icon to 
display a menu of the website. Using the hamburger icon 
can save space on the website, which is especially helpful 
when designing for smaller mobile devices such as cell 
phones. (12)
Hardware as a Service With HaaS, an organization 
outsources its hardware operations needs to the service 
provider. (3)
Hidden Field A type of control found on web forms is 
a hidden field. These are not visible to the viewer, do not 
take up any space on the web page, and can contain only a 
name and a value. (12)
Hierarchical A logically structured database. (13)
Horizontal Check-Off Caption Superior to a 
line caption when information required is routine and 
constant. (12)
Hub Connectivity A cloud-hosted connection aggre-
gation solution that enables customers with large branch 
office networks to consume dedicated cloud connections. 
It is used to connect multiple devices in a network. (16)
Human–Computer Interaction (HCI) The aspect 
of a computer that enables communications and interac-
tions between humans and the computer; the layer of the 
computer between humans and the computer. (1)
Hybrid Approach Appropriate situations for combin-
ing agile methods with SDLC processes is called hybrid 
approach. (6)
ID The attribute list definition for a customer number con-
tains a keyword ID (in uppercase letters). This means that 
the attribute number must appear only once in the XML 
document as an attribute for an element with an ID. (8)
IDREF A document also may have an IDREF attribute, 
which links one element with another that is an ID. (8)
Image Map Image map fields are used to select values 
within an image. The user clicks on a point within an 
image, and the corresponding x- and y-coordinates are sent 
to the program. Image maps are used when creating web 
pages containing maps with instructions to click in a cer-
tain area to view a detailed map of the region. (12)
Infographics The term infographic can mean any graph, 
chart, or picture that communicates the data better than 
words or tables. To the designer, it means visually display-
ing data for the user to see and choosing which type of 
graph, chart, or even which numbers are most appropriate. 
(11)
Information System Utility This approach for evalu-
ating IS can be a comprehensive and fruitful technique for 
measuring the success of a developed system. (16)
Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) With IaaS, also 
called Hardware as a Service (HaaS), an organization 
outsources its hardware operations needs to the service 
provider. (3)
Inheritance The capacity of a derived class to inherit 
all the attributes and behaviors of the base class in object-
oriented analysis and design. Classes can have children; 
the parent class is known as the base class, and the child 
class is called a derived class. (10)
Input Validation Input validation (displaying the item 
the user just typed in) is a way to prevent the user from 
entering the wrong type of data or incorrectly formatted 
data into the file or database. The design is such that the 
user is not permitted to advance to the next input field until 
the problem is corrected. (12)
Intangible Benefits Benefits that accrue to an organi-
zation as a result of a new information system and that 
are difficult to measure, such as improving decision 
making, enhancing accuracy, and becoming more com-
petitive. (3) See also intangible costs, tangible benefits, 
tangible costs.
Intangible Costs Costs that are difficult to estimate and 
may not be known, including losing a competitive edge, 
losing a reputation for innovation, and declining company

## PAGE 585
GLOSSARY     549
image, due to untimely or inaccessible information. (3) See 
also intangible benefits, tangible benefits, tangible costs.
Intelligent Personal Assistants Software agents that 
can accept commands written or spoken from users and 
perform tasks based on that input. (14) See also virtual 
assistants.
Interface Data Flow Entities are usually not shown 
on the child diagrams below Diagram 0. Data flow that 
matches the parent flow is called an interface data flow. (7)
Internal Auditor Study the controls used in the IS to 
make sure they are adequate and are doing what they are 
purported to be doing. lSO test the adequacy of security 
controls. (16)
Interdependent All subsystems in a system are interde-
pendent. They depend on each other to operate and actions 
of each impact the other. (2)
Interrelated All systems and subsystems are interre-
lated. Organizations are composed of smaller, interrelated 
systems (departments, units, divisions, and so on) that 
serve specialized functions. Typical functions include 
accounting, marketing, production, IT support, operations, 
legal, and management. Specialized functions (smaller sys-
tems) are eventually reintegrated through various ways to 
form an effective organizational whole. (2)
Interval Scales In interval scales, the intervals between 
each pair of numbers are equal. Due to this characteristic, 
mathematical operations can be performed on the ques-
tionnaire data, resulting in a more complete analysis. (4)
IS Quality Circles A participatory management tech-
nique that enlists the help of employees in solving prob-
lems related to their own jobs. (16)
Intuitive Navigation Ecommerce websites are com-
monly designed to have “intuitive navigation.” Users need 
to know how to navigate a site without having to learn a 
new interface and without having to explore the website 
before they can find what they want. (14)
Iterations to the First Release The third stage in the 
agile development process is composed of iterations to the 
first release. Typically, these iterations (cycles of testing, 
feedback, and change) are about three weeks in duration. 
You will be pushing yourself to sketch out the entire archi-
tecture of the system, even though it is just in outline or 
skeletal form. One goal is to run customer-written function 
tests at the end of each iteration. (1)
Java An object-oriented programming language that 
allows dynamic applications to be run on the internet.
Join A synchronization in which several events merge into 
one event is called a join. (10)
Joint Application Design (JAD) IBM’s proprietary 
approach to panel interviews conducted with analysts, 
users, and executives to accomplish requirements analysis 
jointly. (4)
Kanban A concept widely used in software development 
that emphasizes visualizing the workflow, keeping work-
in-progress (WIP) as small as possible, reevaluating the 
workflow, reassigning priorities if need be, and striving for 
continual improvement, eliminating bottlenecks and evalu-
ating WIP limits. (6)
Keyboarding The oldest method of data entry, and 
the one with which organization members are most 
familiar. (15)
Layers Dynamic web pages can store information 
through a series of stacked layers (using the cascad-
ing styles z-index property) in the web page design, 
each on top of another. The main web page is the base 
plane, the standard layer of the web page that displays or 
obtains information; other layers below the page are not 
visible. (12)
Leniency Leniency is a problem caused by respondents 
to surveys who are easy raters. A systems analyst can avoid 
the problem of leniency by moving the “average” category 
to the left (or right) of center. (4)
Level 0 Diagram The explosion (or decomposition) of 
a context-level data flow diagram that shows from three to 
nine major processes, important data flows, and data stores 
of the system under study. (7)
Line Captions These captions can be to the left of 
blanks and on the same line, or they can be printed below 
the line on which data will be entered. (12)
Link Testing This testing checks to see whether 
programs that are interdependent work together as 
planned. (16)
Linux Kernel One of the largest open source projects in 
the world, the Linux kernel is a monolithic kernel encom-
passing the software that can be most easily replaced that 
interfaces with all applications that run in user mode such 
as CPU, memory, and inter-process communication. (1)
List Box A list box displays several options that may be 
selected with the mouse. (12)
Live Data Data that have been successfully processed 
through the existing system. This step allows an accu-
rate comparison of the new system’s output with what 
the analyst knows to be correctly processed output, as 
well as a good idea for testing how actual data will be 
handled. (16)
Local Area Network (LAN) The cabling, hardware, 
and software used to connect workstations, computers, and 
file servers located in a confined geographical area (typi-
cally within one building or campus). (16)
Logical Data Flow Diagram A diagram that focuses 
on the business and how the business operates; describes 
the business events that take place and the data required 
and produced by each event. (7) See also data flow dia-
gram, physical data flow diagram.

## PAGE 586
550    GLOSSARY
Logical Security This refers to logical controls in the 
software itself. The logical controls familiar to most users 
are passwords or authorization codes of some sort. When 
used, they permit the user with the correct password to 
enter the system or a particular part of a database. (16)
Logical View A representation of data that resides in an 
enterprise. (13)
Luhn Formula A formula that can be used to identify 
mistakes in data entry. For example, entering an incorrect 
credit card on an ecommerce site. It can be used along with 
other verification methods, such as the credit card expira-
tion date or verification code. (15)
Magnetic Ink Character Recognition 
(MICR) Is done through a machine that reads and 
interprets a single line of material encoded with ink that is 
made up of magnetic particles. (15)
Main Path Each path through various decisions included 
on the use case is a different scenario. The main path 
would be the opening actions such as Logon System, 
Receive Web Form, and so on. (10)
Maintenance Phase A phase of the SDLC in which 
maintaining the information system to improve it or to fix 
problems begins. Maintenance continues throughout the 
life of a system. Some maintenance can be done automati-
cally by connecting to the vendor’s website. (1)
Mark-Sense Forms Forms that allow data entry 
through the use of a scanner that senses where marks have 
been made on special forms. (15)
Mashup A new application created by combining two or 
more web-based APIs together. (14)
Master Files These contain records for a group of 
 entities. (13)
Megamenus Megamenus are large, two-dimensional 
panels you can divide into groups showing options for 
navigation. (11)
Menu A set of options presented to the user of a computer 
application to help them find information or execute a 
function, similar to a menu in a restaurant. (14)
Merge Merges show several events combining to form 
one event. (10)
Message An object in one class can send information to 
an object in another class by using a message, similar to a 
call in a traditional programming language. (10)
Message Boxes Used to warn users and provide 
other feedback messages in a dialog box, often overlap-
ping the display. Message boxes have different formats. 
Each should appear in a rectangular window and should 
clearly spell out the message so that the user knows 
precisely what is happening and what actions are 
 possible. (12)
Metadata A data about the data in a file or database. 
Metadata describes the name given and the length assigned 
to each data item. It also describes the length and composi-
tion of each of the records. (13)
Metatags Metatags are keywords that search engines will 
use to link search requests to your site.
Metaverse A massively scaled and interoperable network 
of real-time rendered 3D virtual worlds which can be expe-
rienced synchronously and persistently by an effectively 
unlimited number of users with an individual sense of 
 presence, and with continuity of data. (Ball, 2021). (14)
Method Overloading Method overloading refers to 
including the same method (or operation) several times in 
a class. (10)
Method Overriding Method overriding (not the same 
as method overloading), is the capability of an object- 
oriented program to have several versions of the same 
method with the same name within a superclass/subclass 
relationship. (10)
Middle Management Middle management forms the 
second, or intermediate, tier of the three-tiered manage-
ment system. Middle managers make short-term planning 
and control decisions about how resources may best be 
allocated to meet organizational objectives. (2)
Mockup An alternative to a wireframe is a mockup. 
Mockups show what the output and input will look like 
before programming the underlying code to make the pro-
gram or application functional. (11)
Moving Average This method is useful because some 
seasonal, cyclical, or random patterns may be smoothed, 
leaving the trend pattern. The principle behind moving 
averages is to calculate the arithmetic mean of data from a 
fixed number of periods; a three-month moving average is 
simply the average of the past three months. (3)
MoSCoW Method A method used by systems analysts 
for requirements prioritization. The analyst asks partici-
pants to put the requirement into one of four “buckets” or 
categories: 1. Must, 2. Should, 3. Could, and 4. Won’t. (4)
Mnemonic Code A device (often using a combina-
tion of letters and symbols) that helps a data entry person 
remember how to enter data correctly or helps the user 
remember how to use the information. (15)
Mini Specs They are a small portion of the total project 
specifications. (9)
Modular Approach This approach involves breaking 
the programming into logical, manageable portions, or 
modules. (16)
Modular Conversion This conversion uses the build-
ing of self-contained, operational subsystems to change 
from old systems to new in a gradual manner. As each 
module is modified and accepted, it is put into use. (16)
Multitouch Gestures Capacitive sensing featuring 
screens controlled with a human finger or a conductive 
stylus) for moving from one screen to another or from

## PAGE 587
GLOSSARY     551
one state to another on the same screen. An example of 
the latter is enlarging a photo by flicking your fingers 
outward. (14)
Natural-Language Interface An interface that per-
mits a user to interact with a computer by speaking or writ-
ing in human language. (14)
Navigation Bars Designed to be consistently displayed 
on the home page as well as at the top and on the left of all 
other pages that comprise the site. Once you have estab-
lished the most useful and most used categories, they can 
be included on all of a website’s pages. (14)
Near Field Communication A way to input data that 
enables secure, contactless payment. A short-range wire-
less connectivity technology that lets NFC-enabled devices 
communicate with each other. (15)
Nested Menus Allows the screen to appear less clut-
tered, which is consistent with good design. They also 
allow users to avoid seeing menu options in which they 
have no interest. (14)
Network Composed of two or more computers that 
are linked in order to share resources (such as print-
ers and CDs), exchange files, or allow electronic 
communications. (13)
Network Decomposition Part of network modeling, 
it provides an overview of the system. Special symbols can 
be used. (16)
Network Modeling A way to show connectivity of 
workstations in a network in some detail. Special symbols 
can be used. (16)
No-Code Platforms for no-code are springing up, and, 
along with these platforms, one can find a range of tutori-
als that will help organizational members who are nonpro-
grammers design and build websites or mobile apps. (6)
Nominal Scales Nominal scales are used to classify 
things. They are the weakest forms of measurement. 
 Generally, all an analyst can do with them is obtain totals 
for each classification. (4)
Nonoperational Prototype This conception of a pro-
totype is that of a nonworking scale model that is set up to 
test certain aspects of the design, called a nonoperational 
prototype. (6)
Normalization The transformation of complex user 
views and data stores to a set of smaller, stable data struc-
tures. Normalized data structures are more easily main-
tained than complex structures. (13)
Notices Similar to alerts and queries for smartphones, 
notices convey information to a user. (14)
Object In the object-oriented approach, a computer repre-
sentation of some real-world thing or event; can have both 
attributes and behaviors. (10)
Object Identifier A unique key for each record in the 
database, not just in a table. (13)
Object Oriented An approach to programming as well 
as analysis and design that differs from traditional proce-
dural programming by examining the objects that are part 
of a system. Each object is a computer representation of an 
actual thing or event. Key object-oriented concepts include 
objects, classes, and inheritance. The Universal Modeling 
Language (UML) is the standard diagramming tool for 
object-oriented modeling. (10)
Object-Oriented (O-O) Systems Analysis and 
Design Object-oriented (O-O) systems analysis and 
design is an approach intended to facilitate the develop-
ment of systems that must change rapidly in response to 
dynamic business environments. (1)
One-Click Navigation A navigational approach where 
users need to know how to navigate a site without  having 
to learn a new interface and without having to explore 
every inch of the website before they can find what they 
want. (14)
On-Site Customer A user who is an expert in the busi-
ness aspect of the systems development work is on-site 
during the agile development process. (6)
Open-Ended Question A type of question used 
in interviews or on surveys that opens up the possi-
ble response set available to respondents. (4) See also 
 Dichotomous question, closed question.
Open Source Communities Loosely organized 
communities of open source software coders and corpora-
tions who collaborate to produce, maintain, and improve 
open-source software, Communities can be categorized 
into four types—ad hoc, standardized, organized, and 
commercial—along six different dimensions: general 
structure, environment, goals, methods, user community, 
and licensing. (1)
Optical Character Recognition (OCR) Lets a 
user read input from a source document (handwritten or 
printed) with an optical scanner rather than off the mag-
netic media. (15)
Option Button Also called a radio button, is used to 
select exclusive choices. By using option buttons, analysts 
can make it clear to users that they must decide among 
options. Choices are again listed to the right of the button, 
usually in some sequence. If there is a commonly selected 
option, it is usually selected as a default when the page 
first displays. Often there is a rectangle, called an option 
group, surrounding the radio buttons. If there are more 
than six option buttons, the designer should consider using 
a list box or a drop-down list box. (12)
Organizational Metaphors A figure of speech that 
asserts one element is another: e.g. an organization is a 
family, society, machine, organism, game, journey, and so 
on. Research shows information systems success may by 
shaped by the predominant organizational metaphor. (16)

## PAGE 588
552    GLOSSARY
Open Source Software (OSS) A development 
model and philosophy of liberating software from certain 
licensing restrictions and publishing its source code, which 
can then be studied, shared, and modified by users and 
programmers, sometimes in proprietary ways for profit as 
long as all licenses are abided by. The Linux operating sys-
tem is an example. (1)
Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) Handles 
complex online queries. (13)
Open System Part of general systems theory; a system 
that freely receives information, energy, people, or raw 
materials as input. Systems are never totally closed or 
totally open but exist on a continuum from more closed to 
more open. (2) See also closed system.
Operational Control Operational control forms the 
bottom tier of three-tiered management. Operations man-
agers make decisions using predetermined rules that have 
predictable outcomes when implemented correctly. They 
make decisions that affect implementation in work sched-
uling, inventory control, shipping, receiving, and control of 
processes such as production. Operations managers over-
see the operating details of the organization. (2)
Operational Feasibility Operational feasibility is 
dependent on the human resources available for the project 
and involves forecasting whether the system will operate 
and be used once it is put into service. (3)
Organizational Boundaries Organizational bounda-
ries exist on a continuum ranging from extremely perme-
able to almost impermeable. To continue to adapt and 
survive, organizations must be able first to import people, 
raw materials, and information through their boundaries 
(inputs) and then to exchange their finished products, ser-
vices, or information with the outside world (outputs). (2)
Organizational Culture An established area of 
research that has grown remarkably in the past decades. 
Just as it is appropriate to think of organizations as 
including many technologies, it is similarly appropri-
ate to see them as hosts to multiple, often competing 
 subcultures. (2)
Orientation Screen orientation changes the number of 
words on a page and also changes the column structure. 
Developers can use different orientations to their advan-
tage. In portrait mode, a calculator app can present a very 
simple calculator, but putting it in landscape mode may 
transform it into a powerful scientific calculator. Giving 
this easy option to a customer is usually welcomed. (11)
Output Bias Output bias is introduced when analysts 
and users make choices about how information is sorted 
for a report. Common sorts include alphabetical, chrono-
logical, and cost. (11)
Output Design Design of webpages, forms, screens, 
and other types of output from information systems. (11)
Package In UML, a grouping of things that can be con-
sidered physical subsystems. Systems are implemented 
and deployed in packages. (10)
Packed Decimal The packed decimal format is com-
monly used to save space on file layouts and for elements that 
require a high level of arithmetic to be performed on them. (8)
Pair Programming A core practice of the agile 
approach in which two programmers who choose to work 
together both do programming, run tests, and talk to one 
another about ways to efficiently and effectively get the 
job done. (6)
Parallel Conversion This refers to running an old sys-
tem and a new system at the same time, in parallel. (16)
Parent Process Each process on Diagram 0 may in turn 
be exploded to create a more detailed child diagram. The 
process on Diagram 0 that is exploded is called the parent 
process. (7)
Partial Dependencies Are those in which all nonkey 
attributes are fully dependent on the primary key. (13)
Partitioning The process of examining a DFD and deter-
mining how it should be divided into collections of manual 
procedures and collections of computer applications. (7)
Payback Payback is useful when a business is growing, 
and volume is a key variable in costs. Payback analysis can 
determine how long it will take for the benefits of the sys-
tem to pay back the costs of developing it. (3)
Patched-Up Prototype The first kind of prototyp-
ing has to do with constructing a system that works but 
is patched up or patched together, called a patched-up 
 prototype. (6)
Performance It refers to a combination of the efficiency 
involved in performing a task and the quality of the work 
produced by the task. (14)
PERT Diagram A tool used to determine critical activi-
ties for a project. It can be used to improve a project sched-
ule and evaluate progress. Also called a network diagram 
in Microsoft Project. PERT stands for Program Evaluation 
Review Technique. (3)
Physical Data Flow Diagram A DFD that shows 
how a system will be implemented, including the hard-
ware, software, people, and files involved. (7) See also 
logical data flow diagram.
Physical Data Structure Using the logical design as a 
basis, the analyst then designs the physical data structures, 
which include additional elements necessary for imple-
menting the system. (8)
Physical Security This refers to securing the computer 
facility, its equipment, and software through physical 
means. It can include controlling access to the computer 
room by means of machine-readable badges, biometric 
systems, or a human sign-in/sign-out system, as well 
as using closed-circuit television cameras to monitor

## PAGE 589
GLOSSARY     553
computer areas, backing up data frequently, and storing 
backups in a fireproof, waterproof area, often at a secure 
off-site location, or using data storage provided by cloud 
vendors. (16)
Physical View This view is involved with how data are 
stored and related, as well as how they are accessed. (13)
Picker A way to allow users to pick a choice from a pre-
determined list, available for developing drop down lists 
for Apple products. A picker includes one or more scrol-
lable list where the selected value appears in darker text in 
the center of the view. (12)
Planned Value Planned value (PV) is the value of the 
work that is to be completed on the project (or, alterna-
tively, the work completed on any task). Because the value 
of the work completed is how much effort and money will 
be put into it, you can think of PV as the budgeted cost of 
work scheduled. (3)
Planning Game A game used in agile development that 
spells out rules that can help formulate an agile develop-
ment team’s relationship with its business customers. (1)
Planning Phase Planning may take only a few days to 
accomplish. In this stage, you and your customers agree 
on a date anywhere from two months to half a year from 
the current date to deliver solutions to their most pressing 
business problems. (1)
Polymorphism Alternative behaviors among derived 
classes in object-oriented approaches. When several 
classes inherit both attributes and behaviors, the behavior 
of a derived class might be different from its base class or 
its sibling-derived classes. (10)
Primary Key A key that uniquely identifies a record. 
(13) See also key, secondary key.
Primary Use Case Use case scenario is a verbal articu-
lation of exceptions to the main behavior described by the 
primary use case. (10)
Primitive Process Processes may or may not be 
exploded, depending on their level of complexity. When a 
process is not exploded, it is said to be functionally primi-
tive and is called a primitive process. (7)
Privacy Directive (EU Cookie Law) A piece of 
legislation governing privacy of data entered into websites, 
which require consent from users before storing, using, 
or retrieving their personal information. Regulates the 
processing of personal data in the form of electronic com-
munications. (14)
Privacy Policy A statement or legal document on a 
corporate ecommerce website that discloses some or all 
of the ways it gathers, uses, discloses, and manages a 
customer or client’s data. Essential to a secure and private 
website. (16)
Private Cloud An internal one that can be realized 
by centralizing the organization’s data centers through 
a privately configured cloud service. More critical data 
linked to strategic aims and corporate intellectual property 
are held in a private cloud. (1)
Probes Follow-up questions used during interviews 
between analysts and users. (4) See also closed question, 
open-ended question, dichotomous question.
Problem Definition A formal statement of a problem, 
including: 1) the issues of the present situation, 2) the 
objectives for each issue, 3) the requirements that must be 
included in all proposed systems, and 4) the constraints 
that limit system development. (3)
Process The activities that transform or change data 
in an information system. They can be either manual or 
automated. In Gane and Sarson notation, signified by a 
rounded rectangle in a data flow diagram. (7)
Process Mining Process mining, is an automated, unob-
trusive process that scans processes found through APIs 
(application programming interfaces) in packages from 
company software products such as SAP, Sales force, and 
Oracle, among others, and identifying where problems 
may exist. (5)
Process Specifications Process specifications are cre-
ated for primitive processes on a data flow diagram as well 
as for some higher-level processes that explode to a child 
diagram. (9)
Product Backlog The product backlog is composed 
of features and other deliverables designers intend for the 
product based on user stories. (6)
Productionizing Phase The phase in agile devel-
opment when the software is released and feedback to 
improve the software product is received. Product releases 
can happen as often as every week. (1)
Productivity Goals As they work with their team 
members on a variety of projects, the team leader will 
acquire acumen for projecting what the team can achieve 
in a specific amount of time and in that way, establish pro-
ductivity goals. (3)
Program Testing The process of executing a program 
with the intent of finding errors. Much of the responsibil-
ity for program testing resides with the original author. 
Systems analysts serve as advisors and coordinators for 
program testing with test data. (16)
Project Charter A written document that describes the 
expected results of a systems project (deliverables) and the 
time frame for delivery; it essentially becomes a contract 
between the chief analyst (or project manager) and his or 
her analysis team with the organizational users requesting 
the new system. (3)
Prototype A model or mock-up of a document, system, 
interface, or display, rather than the actual thing. There 
are four kinds or prototypes we can use in systems design, 
patched-up prototype, nonoperational prototype, first-of-a

## PAGE 590
554    GLOSSARY
series prototype, and selected features prototype. (6) See 
also prototyping.
Prototyping A rapid, interactive process between users 
and analysts to create and refine portions of a new system; 
it can be used as part of the SDLC for requirements deter-
mination or as an alternative to the SDLC. (1) See also 
rapid application development.
Public Cloud An external cloud provider. Sometimes 
the decision to use a public cloud has to do with how criti-
cal it is for the company to control data strategically. (1)
Public Key Infrastructure A way that organizations 
manage security through encryption. An SSL/TSL certifi-
cate for an ecommerce website is a common instance of 
PKI security. (16)
Pull-Down Menu A GUI design element that provides 
an onscreen menu of command options that appear after 
the user selects the command name on a menu bar. (14) 
See also drop-down list.
Purposive Sample A purposive sample is based on 
judgment. The systems analyst chooses a group of indi-
viduals who appear knowledgeable and who are interested 
in the new information system. (5)
Pyramid Structure A structure for sequencing ques-
tions used in interviews that starts with specific, closed 
queries and works down to more generalized ones. (4)
Q-Sort A method used by systems analysts for require-
ments prioritization. It involves sorting a deck of cards 
of requirements into piles. It differs from other methods 
because the participant must place a certain number of 
requirements in each pile. (4)
Quantitative Documents Many quantitative docu-
ments are available for interpretation in any business, and 
they include reports used for decision making, perfor-
mance reports, records, and a variety of forms. (5)
Qualitative Documents Qualitative documents 
include written email messages, memos, signs on bulletin 
boards and in work areas, web pages, procedure manuals, 
and policy handbooks. (5)
Query by Example (QBE) A simple but powerful 
method for implementing queries in database systems, 
such as Microsoft Access. (14)
Quick Response (QR) Codes  A type of optical and 
machine-readable matrix bar code first created in 1994 by 
DENSO WA VE, a Toyota subsidiary. QR codes appear in 
print and on the web and can direct users to encoded web-
sites. (15)
Queries Questions users pose to a database concerning 
data within it. Each query involves an entity, an attribute, 
and a value. (14)
Question-and-Answer Interface The computer 
displays a question to the user on the display and the user 
enters an answer (via a keyboard stroke or a mouse click), 
and the computer then acts on that input information in a 
preprogrammed manner. (14)
Questionnaires The use of questionnaires is an 
 information-gathering technique that allows systems ana-
lysts to study attitudes, beliefs, behavior, and characteris-
tics of several key people in the organization who may be 
affected by the current and proposed systems. (4)
Radio Button A GUI design element that provides a 
round option button in a dialog box. Buttons are mutually 
exclusive because a user can choose only one radio button 
option within the group of options displayed. (12)
Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) Uses 
electromagnetic fields to automatically identify and track 
tags attached to objects. (15)
Record A collection of data items that have something in 
common with the entity described. (13)
Referential Integrity It governs the nature of records 
in a one-to-many relationship. (13)
Regular Expressions Validating a single field is usu-
ally done with a series of IF . . . ELSE statements, but 
there are also pattern validation methods. These patterns 
can be found in the database design (as in Microsoft 
Access) but may be included in programming languages 
such as Perl, JavaScript, and XML schemas. The pat-
terns are called regular expressions and contain symbols 
that represent the type of data that must be present in a 
field. (15)
Relational Data Structure Consists of one or more 
two-dimensional tables, which are referred to as relations. 
The rows of the table represent the records, and the col-
umns contain attributes. (13)
Relationship An association between entities (some-
times referred to as data association); this can take the 
form of one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, or many-
to-many in entity-relationship (E-R) diagrams. (2)
Reliability This measures consistency. If a question-
naire was administered once and then again under the 
same conditions and if the same results were obtained both 
times, the instrument is said to have external consistency. 
If the questionnaire contains subparts and these parts have 
equivalent results, the instrument is said to have internal 
consistency. Both external and internal consistency are 
important. (4)
Repeating Group The existence of many of the same 
elements in a data structure. (8) See also data structure.
Repeating Item An item that repeats in a data structure. (8)
Report File When it is necessary to print a report when 
no printer is available (for example, when the printer is 
busy printing other jobs), a report file is used. (13)
Requirements Prioritization Requirements prioriti-
zation involves the analyst, and client or customer. There 
are six methods widely used for requirements prioritization

## PAGE 591
GLOSSARY     555
to help analysts and customers decide which features 
should be part of the software product. (4)
Requirements Traceability This can be accom-
plished by manually keeping track of requirements in a 
traceability matrix that can be maintained in Microsoft 
Excel. Agile developers may use software such as Jama 
Software to trace requirements. (16)
Responsive Web Design Responsive web design 
means that a website is developed so that it can be viewed 
on any device: a desktop, tablet, or smartphone. (11)
Risk Assessment An essential part of database planning 
where the value of the data and the cost of protecting it are 
weighed against the loss of data or security. (13)
Roll_Over Menu An on-screen function that is trig-
gered when a cursor is placed over (rolled over) a selected 
image or text on a website. (14)
RSS (Real Simple Syndication) Feeds A web 
feed that allows users and applications to access updates to 
websites in a standardized, computer-readable format. (11)
Retrieval The process by which data is selected and 
extracted from a file, a group of files, or a database. (13)
Sampling Systematically selecting representative ele-
ments of a population. Analysts sample hard data, archival 
data, and people during information requirements determi-
nation. (5)
Schedule Performance Index (SPI) A ratio that 
tells the analyst whether a project is falling behind sched-
ule. If the SPI is less than 1.0, it implies that the project or 
task is behind schedule. If the SPI ratio is greater than 1.0, 
the project is ahead of schedule. (3)
Schedule Variance (SV) Tells the analyst whether the 
project is taking more time than planned. If so, it will have 
a negative impact on project costs. Once again, if SV is 
negative, then it is taking longer than planned to perform 
the work, and the amount is the cost overage. If SV is posi-
tive, then it is taking less time than planned, and the amount 
indicates how much the project is under budget. (3)
Scope of the System The context-level data flow dia-
gram is one way to show the scope of the system, or what 
is to be included in the system. The external entities are 
outside the scope and something over which the system 
has no control. (2)
Scrum Development teams begin a project with a high-
level plan that can be changed on the fly as the “game” 
progresses. Scrum is an agile software development 
method suitable for complex projects. A limited number 
of features or tasks are selected for completion in a sprint, 
typically lasting two to four weeks. The result of this is a 
potentially shippable product. Once the sprint ends, the 
process begins again with new priorities and features to 
be finished in the next sprint. (1) See also sprint and sprint 
cycle.
Scrum Planning Poker Scrum planning poker is a 
way to help the team determine estimates for completing 
the features that arise from user stories. (6)
Second Normal Form (2NF) A stage of data nor-
malization in which an analyst ensures that all nonkey 
attributes are fully dependent on the primary key. All par-
tial dependencies are removed and placed in another rela-
tion. (13) See also first normal form, third normal form.
Secondary Key A key that cannot uniquely identify a 
record; can be used to select a group of records that belong 
to a set. (13)
Security Security is critical to the functioning of organi-
zational information systems, and security is a challenge 
for everyone involved in systems development. (1)
Selected-Features Prototype A fourth conception of 
prototyping concerns building an operational model that 
includes some, but not all, of the features that the final sys-
tem will have, called a selected-features prototype. (6)
Self-Validating Codes Method for ensuring the accu-
racy of data, particularly identification numbers, is to use a 
check digit in the code itself. (15)
Sequence Code It is a number assigned arbitrarily to 
something if it needs to be numbered. It has no relation to 
the data themselves. (15)
Sequence Diagram In UML, a diagram that illustrates 
a succession of interactions between object instances over 
time. Often used to illustrate the processing described in 
use case scenarios. (10)
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) An archi-
tecture in which individual software services that are 
unassociated or only loosely coupled to one another are 
available as applications or parts of applications to users, 
often using the web as a platform. (16)
Short Releases Short releases means that the develop-
ment team compresses the time between releases of the 
product. Rather than release a full-blown version in a year, 
using the short release practice, the team will tackle the 
most important features first, release that system or prod-
uct, and then improve it later. (6)
Significant-Digit Subset Code Code that uses sub-
groups of digits to describe a product or numbers that are 
arbitrarily assigned. An advantage is that it makes it possible 
to locate items that belong to a certain group or class. (15)
Simple Random Sample The sample must come 
from a numbered list of the population to ensure that each 
document or person in the population has an equal chance 
of being selected. (5)
Simple Ranking A method used by systems analysts 
for requirements prioritization. A systems analyst asks 
all interested parties to rank-order a list of requirements 
under consideration by assigning a 1 to the most desirable 
requirement and a 10 to the least desirable. (4)

## PAGE 592
556    GLOSSARY
Site Map A way to improve navigational efficiency 
of website design. A representation of the pages to be 
included on a website, the menu, and navigation structure, 
and how the pages link. This should be included on the 
home page. (14)
Six Sigma A culture built on quality; the goal is to elimi-
nate all defects. (16)
Skeuomorphic Design Web design featuring 3-D, 
realistic images with shadows (see in contrast FLAT WEB 
DESIGN). (11)
Slack A proprietary business social media platform for the 
workplace that incorporates email, chatrooms, and direct 
messaging. Each work team has channels that are like big 
chatrooms that can be public or semipublic. (2)
Sliders Used in designing websites to change data that 
have a continuous range of values, giving users more con-
trol when choosing values. Moving the slider in one direc-
tion or the other (either left/right or up/down) increases or 
decreases the values. (12)
Socioemotional Leader One person will emerge who 
is concerned with the social relationships among group 
members. (3)
Social Media Designing output and content for social 
media has become a career in its own right, but systems 
analysts may be faced with those design tasks as part 
of their overall information systems design work for an 
organization. (11)
Source The external entity (also called an entity) in a data 
flow diagram. This is also called a destination for data. It 
is treated as external to the system being described. (7)
Smartphones A cellular telephone with an integrated 
computer and other features such as an operating system, 
web browsing, and the ability to run software applications. 
Designers can use alerts notices, and badges when design-
ing output for smartphones. (14)
Software as a Service (SaaS) One of the compo-
nents of cloud computing. Software distributed by a third-
party that hosts applications for users and makes them 
accessible over the internet. (3)
Special Character A type of value of the data item. (13)
Specialty Form Business forms prepared by a stationer 
or professional printer. Examples of stationers’ specialty 
forms are multiple-part forms that are used to create 
instant triplicates of data, continuous-feed forms that run 
through the printer without intervention, and perforated 
forms that leave a stub behind as a record when they are 
separated. (12)
Spin Buttons They are used to change data that have 
a continuous range of values, giving users more control 
when choosing values. (12)
Sprint and Sprint Cycle In agile software develop-
ment methodology, a sprint cycle has development teams 
working on limited features, lasts two to four weeks, with 
the result of a potentially shippable product. (6) See also 
Scrum.
Sprint Backlog A sprint backlog is created to select 
the user stories that need to be completed soon. Stories are 
deliverables the team accomplishes. (6)
Sprint Review Meeting The sprint review meeting is 
a retrospective in which the team articulates accomplish-
ments and lessons learned. By stating what went well and 
what did not, the team can improve the process in anticipa-
tion of the next sprint. (6)
State States separate events, such as an order that is 
waiting to be filled, and events separate states, such as an 
Order Received event or an Order Complete event. (10)
Statechart Diagram In UML, a diagram that enables 
further refinement of requirements. (10)
Stakeholders The people involved in the first phase are 
the users, analysts, and systems managers coordinating 
the project. We discuss them as stakeholders in the SDLC 
process. (1)
Stickiness A property of web pages, particularly dis-
cussed in ecommerce, that indicates how well the pages 
draw and hold customers. Features that increase the sticki-
ness of a web page are those that entice customers to stay 
on the page longer, help them to navigate back to the page 
if they click on a link, and increase the likelihood they will 
complete a purchase. (11)
Storage as a Service (STaaS) The practice of using 
public cloud storage resources to store your data. (16)
Storage Area Networks (SANs) A network of 
storage devices that can be accessed by multiple serv-
ers or computers, providing a shared pool of storage 
space. (16)
Stories They originate in the workplace, and many are 
shared with, and repeated by, coworkers. Although time-
consuming, listening to stories rather than getting brief 
answers to interview questions may be more rewarding for 
the analyst seeking information requirements. (4)
Storyboarding In developing a website (or any app) a 
storyboard could be used to show the differences between 
screens. It can show, screen by screen, how a visitor to 
a website would use the website to find the information 
desired. Storyboards can be developed using Microsoft 
PowerPoint and many other applications. (11)
Stratified Samples Stratified samples are perhaps 
the most important type of sample for a systems analyst. 
Stratification is the process of identifying subpopulations, 
or strata, and then selecting objects or people for sampling 
in these subpopulations. (5)
Strategic Management Strategic management is the 
third level of three-tiered management control. Strategic 
managers look outward from the organization to the future,

## PAGE 593
GLOSSARY     557
making decisions that will guide middle and operations 
managers in the months and years ahead. (2)
String Testing Checks to see whether programs that are 
interdependent work together as planned. (16)
Structural Records Entries, CUSTOMER NAME, 
ADDRESS, and TELEPHONE, are groups of elements or 
structural records. (8)
Structure Chart A tool for designing a modular, top-
down system consisting of rectangular boxes and connect-
ing arrows. (16) See also control flag, data couple.
Structured Decisions Decisions made in a company 
that are routine, repetitive, and follow a predetermined set 
of steps. They do not necessarily involve a human decision 
maker. When process logic involves formulas or iteration, 
or when structured decisions are not complex, an appropri-
ate technique for analyzing the decision process is the use 
of structured English. (9)
Structured English A technique for analyzing struc-
tured decisions based on structured logic and simple 
 English statements, such as add, multiply, and move. (9)
Structured Observation of the Environment 
(STROBE) A systematic observational method for 
classifying and interpreting organizational elements that 
influence decision making. Based on mise-en-scène film 
criticism. (5)
Structured Query Language (SQL) A popular 
language for implementing queries. (14)
Structured Walkthrough A systematic peer review 
of a system’s programming and overall development that 
points out problems and allows a programmer or an ana-
lyst to make suitable changes. (16)
Stylus A pen-shaped instrument whose tip position on a 
computer monitor can be detected. Provides more control 
of input than a finger. (14)
Supply Chain Management An organization’s effort 
to integrate its suppliers, distributors, and customer man-
agement requirements into one unified process. Ecommerce 
applications can improve supply chain management. (15)
Swimlanes Zones used in activity diagrams to indicate 
partitioning; can show which activities are done on which 
platform and by which user group; can also depict system 
logic. (10)
Synchronization Bar A long, flat rectangle represents 
a synchronization bar. (10)
Synchronous Messages Synchronous messages, 
which are calls to functions or subroutines. The calling 
object stops and waits for control to be returned to it, along 
with an optional message. (10)
System A collection of subsystems that are interrelated 
and interdependent, working together to accomplish pre-
determined goals and objectives. All systems have input, 
processes, output, and feedback. Examples are a computer 
information system and an organization. (2) See also 
closed system, open system.
Systematic Sampling In the simplest method of prob-
ability sampling, systematic sampling, the systems analyst 
would, for example, choose to interview every kth person 
on a list of company employees. (5)
Systems Analysis and Design Systems analysis and 
design is a systematic approach to identifying problems, 
opportunities, and objectives; to analyzing human- and 
computer-generated information flows in organizations; 
and to designing computerized information systems to 
solve problems. (1)
Systems Analyst A person who systematically assesses 
how businesses function by examining the inputting and 
processing of data and the outputting of information with 
the intent of improving organizational processes and the 
quality of work life for users. (1)
Systems Consultant A consultant to people and their 
businesses who may be hired specifically to address infor-
mation systems issues within a business. (1)
Systems Development Life Cycle (SDLC)  
A seven-phase approach to systems analysis and design 
that holds that systems are best developed through the use 
of a specific cycle of analyst and user activities. (1)
Systems Proposal A written proposal that summarizes 
a systems analyst’s work in a business up to the current 
point and includes recommendations and alternatives to 
solve the identified systems problems. One option always 
provided to the client is to “Do nothing and maintain the 
status quo.”(3)
Table Captions A short block of text that gives infor-
mation about the table accompanying it. (12)
Table File A file that contains data used to calculate 
more data or performance measures. They are usually read 
only by a program. (13)
Tablets A wireless touch screen personal computer (PC) 
that is smaller than a notebook but larger than a smart-
phone. (14)
Tangible Benefits Advantages measurable in dollars 
that accrue to an organization through the use of informa-
tion systems. (3) See also intangible benefits, intangible 
costs, tangible costs.
Tangible Costs The costs in dollars that can be accu-
rately projected by a systems analyst, including the cost 
of computers, resources, analysts’ and programmer’s time, 
and other employees’ salaries, to develop a new system. 
(3) See also intangible benefits, intangible costs, tangible 
benefits.
Tasks A piece of work that must be done. Many tasks 
make up a work breakdown structure in a project. (14)
Task Leader The person who emerges to lead members 
to accomplish tasks. (3) See also socioemotional leader.

## PAGE 594
558    GLOSSARY
Task Mining Task mining is an unobtrusive desktop 
capture of the tasks performed by an organization’s 
employees. Task mining makes it possible to see what indi-
viduals are doing. (5)
Team Norms Team norms are collective expectations, val-
ues, and ways of behaving. A team’s norms belong to it and 
will not necessarily transfer from one team to another. (3)
Team Process The interaction of the systems analysis 
team members through which norms of behavior change 
over time. (3)
Temporal Events Events which occur at a predeter-
mined time. These usually do not involve an actor or any 
external event. (10)
Technical Feasibility Technical feasibility includes 
evaluating the ability of computer hardware and software 
to handle workloads adequately. (3)
Test Data Data which has been specifically identified for 
use in tests, typically of a computer program. (16)
Test for Class or Composition The test for class or 
composition validity checks to see that data fields that are 
supposed to be exclusively composed of numbers do not 
include letters, and vice versa. (15)
Test for Comparison with Stored Data The test 
for validity of input data that we consider is one comparing 
it with data that the computer has already stored. (15)
Test for Correct Field Length A validity test that 
checks input to ensure it is of the correct length for the 
field. (15)
Test for Invalid Values The test for checking input for 
invalid values works if there are only a few valid values. 
This test is not feasible for situations in which values are 
neither restricted nor predictable. This kind of test is useful 
for checking responses where data are divided into a lim-
ited number of classes. (15)
Test for Missing Data A validity test that examines 
data to see if there are any missing items. (15)
Test for Range or Reasonableness Validity tests 
for range or reasonableness are really commonsense meas-
ures of input that answer the question of whether data fall 
within an acceptable range or whether they are reasonable 
within predetermined parameters. (15)
Text Analytics Software that can analyze unstructured 
qualitative data from any source, including transcripts of 
interviews, written reports, or customers’ communication 
collected through email, wikis, blogs, chat rooms, and 
other social networking sites. (5)
Text Box A rectangle represents a text box and is used to 
outline data-entry and display fields. A text box should be 
large enough to accommodate all the characters that must 
be entered. Each text box should have a caption to the left, 
identifying what is to be entered or what is displayed in the 
box. (12)
Third Design Space Research team identified and 
described new collaboration between corporate developers 
and communities of developers as the Third Design Space. 
It is a metaphorical, not actual physical, space. Participants 
in the Third Design Space together make a new design 
environment happen; they create new design associations 
and circulate shared design resources. Their collaborations 
result in new, shared software and innovative software 
development processes. (1)
Third Normal Form (3NF) A form in which any 
transitive dependencies are removed. A transitive depend-
ency is one in which nonkey attributes are dependent on 
other nonkey attributes. (13) See also first normal form, 
second normal form.
Throughput Throughput is the average number of items 
that move from the “backlog” column to the “done” col-
umn in a certain period of time or cycle. (6)
Top-Down Approach Drawing data flow diagrams 
involves moving from the general to the specific movement 
of data. (7)
Top-Down Design This design allows a systems ana-
lyst to ascertain overall organization objectives first, as 
well as to ascertain how they are best met in an overall 
system. (16)
Total Quality Management (TQM) A manage-
ment approach that seeks to provide long-term success by 
providing unparalleled customer satisfaction through the 
constant delivery of quality IT services. (16)
Touch-Sensitive Screens Allows a user to use a finger 
to activate the display. (14)
Transaction Data Store Links two processes tempo-
rarily and stores the data temporarily. (7)
Transaction File It is used to enter changes that update 
the master file and produce reports. (13)
Transitive Dependency A dependency in which non-
key attributes are dependent on other nonkey attributes. (13)
Two_Dimensional Bar Codes Matrix bar code 
squares are two-dimensional bar codes. They are still opti-
cal, machine-readable codes, and they take up much less 
space than the older linear bar codes. (15)
Two-Factor Authentication Also known as two-step 
verification or dual-factor authentication security process 
where users give two unique authentication factors to ver-
ify their authorization to access a system or software. (16)
Two-Step Verification A process that involves two 
authentication methods performed one after the other to 
verify that someone or something requesting access is who 
or what they are declared to be. (16)
Three-Factor Authentication Is a security process 
where users will need to possess a physical token and a pass-
word, used along with biometric data, instances of which are 
retinal scans, voiceprints, and scans of fingerprints. (16)

## PAGE 595
GLOSSARY     559
Unicode Character set, which includes all standard lan-
guage symbols and has room for 65,535 characters. (15)
Unified Modeling Language (UML) A language 
that provides a standardized set of tools to document the 
object-oriented analysis and design of a software system. (1)
Unnormalized Relation Often a relation derived from 
the user view or data store. A relation that contains repeat-
ing values. (13)
Update Anomaly It results when a change to one attrib-
ute value either causes the database to contain inconsistent 
data or causes multiple records to need changing. (13)
Urgent/Important Matrix A method used by systems 
analysts for requirements prioritization. A matrix is drawn 
on a whiteboard. The horizonal x-axis is meant to show 
urgency and the vertical y-axis represents importance. (4)
URL Filtering Products Software that enables organ-
izations to restrict the websites and content that employees 
can access. (16)
Usability A way for designers to evaluate the systems 
and interfaces they create, with an eye toward addressing 
as many HCI concerns as they can as thoroughly as pos-
sible. (14)
Use Case In UML, a sequence of transactions in a sys-
tem; the purpose is to produce something of value to an 
actor in the system; focuses on what the system does rather 
than on how it does it. The use case model is based on the 
interactions and relationships of individual use cases. In a 
use case, an actor using the system initiates an event that 
begins a related series of interactions in the system. (2)
Use Case Diagram A use case diagram can be used 
as part of the systems development life cycle (SDLC) 
approach or in agile modeling methodology. The word 
use is pronounced as a noun (“yoos”) rather than a verb 
(“yooz”). (2)
Use Case Scenario This scenario is a verbal articula-
tion of exceptions to the main behavior described in the 
primary use case when developing UML diagrams. (2)
User Experience (UX) Design A customer-first 
method for designing software that observes the behavior 
of customers and enhances customer satisfaction and loy-
alty by improving usability and ease of use. UX is also a 
design culture that emphasizes a good experience for the 
user over maximizing short-term profit. (14)
User Involvement with Prototyping The users’ 
role in prototyping can be summed up in two words: hon-
est involvement. Without user involvement with prototyp-
ing, there is little reason to prototype. (6)
User Stories Part of the agile approach, a user story 
captures a requirement from an end user perspective. It 
is a brief story that is short and easy to comprehend, and 
services as reminder to developers to hold conversations 
about the user requirements in user stories. (6)
Validating Input Testing input received by the applica-
tion for compliance against a standard defined within the 
application. There are several ways to validate input. (15)
Validity On a survey, the degree to which a question 
measures what the analyst intends for it to measure. (4)
Varchar Variable character data may contain any number 
of characters up to a limit set by the database software. 
When using varchar, specifying the length is optional. (8)
Vertical Balancing The primary rule for creating child 
diagrams is vertical balancing, which dictates that a child 
diagram cannot produce output or receive input that the 
parent process does not also produce or receive. (7)
Vertical Check-Off Caption One of the types of 
check-off captions, which is superior when response 
options are necessarily restricted. (12)
Vendor Support Support services include routine and 
preventive maintenance of hardware, specified response 
time (within six hours, next working day, and so on) in 
case of emergency equipment breakdowns, loan of equip-
ment in the event that hardware must be permanently 
replaced or off-site repair is required, and in-house training 
or off-site group seminars for users. (3)
Video-Calling Communicating with one or more people 
using a smartphone, mobile device, webcam, and so on to 
transmit and receive both audio and video. (4)
Video-Conferencing A system that allows people in 
different parts of the world to have a meeting by communi-
cating to each other using video screens. (4)
Virtual Assistants Software agents that can accept 
commands written or spoken from users and perform 
tasks based on that input. (14) See also intelligent personal 
assistants.
Virtual Enterprises Virtual enterprises use networks 
of computers and communications technology to bring 
people with specific skills together electronically to work 
on projects that are not physically located in the same 
place. Information technology enables coordination of 
these remote team members. (2)
Virtual Reality (VR) An artificial, totally computer-
generated world. (14)
Virtual Teams Virtual teams often spring up in already-
established organizations; in some instances, however, 
organizations of remote workers have been able to succeed 
without the traditional investment in a physical facility. (2)
Voice Recognition The ability of a machine or program 
to receive and interpret dictation or to understand and carry 
out spoken commands. (14)
Walled Garden An environment that controls the user’s 
access to network-based content and services. The walled 
garden directs the user’s navigation within particular areas 
to enable access to a selection of material or prevent access 
to other material. (11)

## PAGE 596
560    GLOSSARY
Web Pages A hypertext document on the web. Webpages 
are delivered by a web server to the user and displayed in a 
web browser. (11)
Web 2.0 Technologies Collaborative technologies 
such as blogs, email, and chat rooms that can be added to 
websites to improve interaction and collaboration. (11)
Web-Based Form-Fill Interfaces Displays fields 
that contain data items or parameters that need to be com-
municated to the user. (14)
Whole/Part Relationship In object-oriented analysis 
and design, a whole/part relationship occurs when one 
class represents a whole object and other classes represent 
parts of that object. The whole acts as a container for the 
parts. (10)
Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) A system 
of breaking down a project into simpler tasks, usually by 
decomposition. A WBS can be either product or process 
oriented. Information systems projects tend to be process 
oriented. (3)
Wide Area Network (W AN) A large computer 
network that connects groups of computers over large dis-
tances. (16)
Wikis Links to social networks on which the company has 
a presence, and tagging (also called social bookmarking) 
that provides useful pointers to online resources such as 
websites, content on corporate intranets, corporate docu-
ments, or photos that are relevant to the organization and 
to users. (11)
Wireframing Page design can be accomplished using a 
process called wireframing, which shows only the basics. 
There is no color, no type style. Graphics are shown as a 
simple box with an X drawn in it. (11)
Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) A net-
work of computers located together, based on radio trans-
missions rather than wired connections. (16)
Workforce Analytics An advanced set of data analysis 
tools and metrics for comprehensive workforce perfor-
mance measurement and improvement. (5)
Work Files Temporary files usually called transaction 
files, work files, or report files. (13)
Work Management System A new work manage-
ment system that addresses this concern is Jira. Jira pic-
tures work as a collaborative process and addresses three 
types of work management situations through the use 
of templates: In Jira, there are templates for kanban and 
Scrum. In addition, Jira has templates for bug tracking. (6)
XML Schemas A tool for precisely defining the content 
of an XML document; may include the exact number of 
times an element can occur, the type of data within ele-
ments, limits on the data, and the number of places to the 
left and right of a decimal number. (8)
Zoned Decimal The zoned decimal format is used for 
printing and displaying data. (8)
Zoom Fatigue The tiredness, worry, or burn-
out associated with overusing virtual platforms of 
communication. (4)
